title: Licensed Practical and Licensed Vocational Nurses
keyDrivers: Licensed practical nurses must be physically present to provide hands-on patient care, administering medications, changing dressings, and monitoring patients in unpredictable clinical settings that robots cannot navigate reliably. They carry real legal accountability through their nursing license and patients deeply value having a trusted human caregiver. AI will likely free them from charting and documentation burdens, letting them spend more time on direct care, while growing healthcare demand means more need for their skills, not less.

taskData.full: Supervise nurses' aides or assistants.
Observe patients, charting and reporting changes in patients' conditions, such as adverse reactions to medication or treatment, and taking any necessary action.
Answer patients' calls and determine how to assist them.
Measure and record patients' vital signs, such as height, weight, temperature, blood pressure, pulse, or respiration.
Administer prescribed medications or start intravenous fluids, noting times and amounts on patients' charts.

Provide basic patient care or treatments, such as taking temperatures or blood pressures, dressing wounds, treating bedsores, giving enemas or douches, rubbing with alcohol, massaging, or performing catheterizations.
Evaluate nursing intervention outcomes, conferring with other healthcare team members as necessary.
Work as part of a healthcare team to assess patient needs, plan and modify care, and implement interventions.

Provide medical treatment or personal care to patients in private home settings, such as cooking, keeping rooms orderly, seeing that patients are comfortable and in good spirits, or instructing family members in simple nursing tasks.
Record food and fluid intake and output.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI is moving into documentation and charting work - the administrative layer of nursing that sits around direct patient care - which reduces time burden but also starts to overlap with some of what LPNs do between patient interactions [2]. Remote patient monitoring tools are automating some aspects of vital sign tracking and early deterioration alerts, which were traditionally LPN responsibilities in long-term care settings. The structural risk is not job elimination but role compression: as AI handles more paperwork and monitoring, the work that remains is increasingly the hands-on care that requires physical presence.
risks.statLabel: of healthcare facilities now use AI-assisted documentation tools [4]

opportunities.body: This role scores a 4 out of 5 on physical presence requirements - medication administration, wound care, catheterization, and direct patient observation require someone in the room [2]. The physical care delivery tasks that define LPN work have no observed AI activity, and there is no credible near-term path for AI to perform them. BLS projects 6% employment growth through 2034, driven by aging population demand for long-term care and home health services where LPNs are the primary workforce.
opportunities.statLabel: projected annual job openings for LPNs [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The work most protected from AI is hands-on patient care - the physical tasks that require presence, touch, and real-time clinical judgment during a patient interaction. Build depth in patient assessment skills and complex care settings (long-term care, home health, rehabilitation) where LPN demand is growing fastest and AI tools are least embedded [2]. Over the next six months, get comfortable using AI-assisted documentation tools to reduce charting time - not because the skill is threatened, but because freeing time from paperwork means more patient-facing hours.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: LPN programs typically take 12 to 18 months and lead directly to licensure - it is one of the faster pathways into clinical healthcare [2]. The role is in highest demand in long-term care facilities, home health agencies, and physician offices, and the aging population creates structural tailwinds that are independent of AI trends. Clinical skills, not credential stacking, are what hiring managers want to see - prioritize programs with strong hands-on clinical hours.

quotes.quote: The nurses who thrive as AI tools come into clinical workflows are the ones who stay anchored in what AI can't replicate — physical assessment, therapeutic presence, and patient advocacy. Document with the tools, but own the clinical picture yourself.
quotes.attribution: Health Affairs, 'AI in Nursing: Frontline Perspectives on Clinical Decision Support'

quotes.quote: The nursing workforce needs to develop competencies in evaluating AI-generated clinical recommendations, understanding algorithm limitations, and advocating for patients when automated systems produce outputs that conflict with bedside observation.

LPN programs are one of the fastest entry points into clinical healthcare — and the facilities hiring most aggressively are long-term care and home health, where the patient population is growing and the work is deeply hands-on. Start there, build clinical hours, and credential up from a position of practice.
quotes.attribution: American Nurse Journal, 'Workforce Pathways in Long-Term Care'

quotes.quote: ANA emphasizes that foundational nursing competencies — caring presence, clinical judgment, patient advocacy — are the durable core of the profession, and that new entrants who combine these with comfort using AI documentation and monitoring tools will be most competitive as facilities modernize.

careerCluster.title: Nurse Practitioner
careerCluster.fit: LPNs and LVNs already handle patient monitoring, medications, and direct care, but Nurse Practitioner is a clear step up that requires a graduate degree and gives you the authority to diagnose, create treatment plans, and prescribe independently.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge program (LPN-to-RN)
Complete MSN or DNP with NP specialization
Pass AANP or ANCC NP certification exam

careerCluster.title: Registered Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle vitals, medications, charting, and direct patient care, so the day-to-day work overlaps heavily, but RN is a step up — it requires a higher credential and gives you broader clinical authority, independent judgment, and supervisory responsibility over other nurses.
steps: Gain 1 year LPN clinical experience
Enroll in an LPN-to-RN bridge program
Pass the NCLEX-RN

careerCluster.title: Critical Care Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already monitor vitals, administer medications, and observe patient changes, but critical care nursing is a step up that requires an RN license and the ability to independently interpret complex data and manage life-threatening emergencies.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge LPN-to-RN program
Gain ICU or ER experience as new RN
Pass CCRN certification exam

careerCluster.title: Clinical Nurse Specialist
careerCluster.fit: LPN/LVN work gives you hands-on patient care and clinical observation skills that carry over, but the CNS role is a clear step up — it requires a master's degree, independent clinical judgment in a specialty, and responsibility for teaching other nurses and shaping care standards.
steps: Earn RN licensure via bridge program
Complete MSN with CNS specialty track
Obtain CNS certification from AACN or NACNS

careerCluster.title: Psychiatric Nurse
careerCluster.fit: Your hands-on patient care and medication administration experience carries over, but this is a significant step up that requires a master's or doctoral degree, RN licensure, and the authority to independently diagnose and prescribe.
steps: Earn RN license via ADN or BSN program
Complete MSN or DNP in psychiatric-mental health
Obtain PMHNP-BC certification from ANCC

careerCluster.title: Acute Care Nurse
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle vitals, medications, and patient monitoring, but Acute Care Nurses is a step up that requires an RN license and adds independent diagnosis, prescribing authority, and managing life-threatening conditions.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge LPN-to-RN program
Obtain ACNPC-AG or CCRN certification
Log clinical hours in ICU or emergency settings

careerCluster.title: Nurse Anesthetist
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already monitor vitals, administer medications, and observe patient responses, but Nurse Anesthetist is a significant step up requiring advanced clinical training, a master's or doctoral degree, and independent authority over anesthesia care.
steps: Earn a BSN, then RN license via NCLEX-RN
Gain 1-3 years ICU nursing experience
Complete accredited Nurse Anesthesia program (CRNA)

careerCluster.title: Nurse Midwife
careerCluster.fit: LPNs already handle patient monitoring, vitals, and basic care, but Nurse Midwife is a clear step up that requires an RN license first, then a graduate-level midwifery degree with specialized obstetric training.
steps: Earn RN license via bridge program (LPN-to-RN)
Complete accredited CNM graduate program (MSN or DNP)
Pass AMCB Certified Nurse-Midwife exam

careerCluster.title: Postsecondary Nursing Teacher
careerCluster.fit: Your hands-on clinical experience is real and directly useful in a teaching role, but this is a step up that requires a bachelor's or master's degree in nursing plus a shift from doing patient care to evaluating and instructing others who are learning to do it.
steps: Earn BSN or MSN from accredited nursing program
Apply for adjunct clinical instructor position
Obtain state nursing faculty certification if required

careerCluster.title: Nursing Assistant
careerCluster.fit: An LPN moving to a nursing assistant role is a step down — you already know the clinical environment and patient care routines, but the job scope narrows to basic ADL support with no medication or assessment responsibilities.
steps: No new credential required
Notify employer of LPN license for higher pay grade
Expect reduced scope and pay

title: Air Traffic Controllers
jobTitles: Air Traffic Control Specialist/Certified Professional Controller (ATC Specialist/CPC)
keyDrivers: Air traffic controllers make life-or-death decisions in real time under ambiguous, rapidly changing conditions, the kind of high-stakes judgment where pattern-matching alone isn't enough. Regulations require a certified human to be responsible for separation and sequencing of aircraft, and society is unlikely to remove that requirement anytime soon. AI will likely serve as a powerful decision-support tool, making controllers more effective rather than replacing them, especially as air traffic volume grows.

taskData.full: Transfer control of departing flights to traffic control centers and accept control of arriving flights.
Inform pilots about nearby planes or potentially hazardous conditions, such as weather, speed and direction of wind, or visibility problems.
Monitor or direct the movement of aircraft within an assigned air space or on the ground at airports to minimize delays and maximize safety.
Monitor aircraft within a specific airspace, using radar, computer equipment, or visual references.
Direct ground traffic, including taxiing aircraft, maintenance or baggage vehicles, or airport workers.
Issue landing and take-off authorizations or instructions.
Direct pilots to runways when space is available or direct them to maintain a traffic pattern until there is space for them to land.
Contact pilots by radio to provide meteorological, navigational, or other information.
Maintain radio or telephone contact with adjacent control towers, terminal control units, or other area control centers to coordinate aircraft movement.
Determine the timing or procedures for flight vector changes.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The long-term structural risk is not AI automation of the controller role but a shrinking pipeline of available positions. BLS projects employment to decline slightly as some ground coordination work is automated and operational efficiency improvements reduce staffing requirements at smaller facilities [2]. The career entry barrier is among the highest of any occupation: FAA Academy training is required, most hiring occurs before age 31, and the path runs almost exclusively through FAA or military experience.
risks.statLabel: fewer ATC positions projected by 2034 as NextGen automation consolidates ground coordination [2]

opportunities.body: Air traffic control scores a 5 out of 5 on both novel real-time judgment and real-time response requirements - every traffic situation involves unique combinations of aircraft, weather, and runway conditions that require split-second decisions with no tolerance for error. FAA regulations require human controllers to be the final authority on aircraft separation, and there is no regulatory pathway for AI to operate in this role without human oversight. Federal employment with strong union protections and a median salary above $137, 000 reflects the difficulty of replacing this work [2].
opportunities.statLabel: of ATC decisions require real-time human judgment with no AI override [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The core skill - maintaining situational awareness across multiple simultaneous aircraft movements and making real-time separation decisions - is the most structurally protected work in this occupation and shows no AI footprint in usage data. Build proficiency with the NextGen automation tools that handle flight data and ground coordination, so that the human workload stays focused on the judgment layer rather than data processing [2]. Over the next several years, controller experience in high-traffic facilities or TRACON environments carries the most career leverage as smaller facilities see more automation of procedural work.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The path into air traffic control runs almost entirely through the FAA Academy at Oklahoma City or through military ATC training - there is no civilian community college or online certification route [2]. Most FAA hiring has an upper age cutoff around 31 and requires passing a biographical assessment and medical exam before Academy selection. The career is demanding but highly compensated and structurally protected from AI in the near term. Military ATC service is the most reliable alternative pathway for those who miss the FAA hiring window.

quotes.quote: NextGen and TBFM are handling more of the routine sequencing — which means the cognitive load controllers carry now is weighted more heavily toward exception management and conflict resolution. Invest time in understanding what the automation is doing so you can catch it when it's wrong.
quotes.attribution: FAA, 'Human Factors in NextGen Automation: Controller Perspectives'

quotes.quote: FAA's NextGen modernization requires controllers to work alongside increasingly sophisticated automation tools — DataComm digital clearances, Performance-Based Navigation, and time-based flow management. Proficiency with these systems is becoming a baseline expectation, not an advanced skill.

The FAA Academy pipeline is narrow by design — and that constraint is a feature for people already in it. Candidates who come in with strong spatial reasoning, instrument familiarity, and the ability to hold multiple tracks in working memory will move through faster. The job is cognitively demanding in ways automation is not replacing.
quotes.attribution: FAA, Air Traffic Organization Workforce Plan

quotes.quote: FAA's Collegiate Training Initiative (CTI) schools offer a structured path into the ATC pipeline for candidates without military experience. Completing a CTI program and passing the biographical assessment significantly improves chances of an Academy selection in a field where the application pool is large relative to openings.

careerCluster.title: Airline Pilot or Flight Engineer
careerCluster.fit: Air traffic controllers already understand airspace, radio communication, and flight procedures deeply, but becoming a pilot means switching from directing aircraft to physically flying one, which requires flight hours, new certifications, and hands-on stick-and-rudder skills.
steps: Earn FAA Private Pilot Certificate
Accumulate 1,500 flight hours for ATP certificate
Pass ATP written and checkride exams

careerCluster.title: Commercial Pilot
careerCluster.fit: Air traffic controllers already understand airspace, weather conditions, radio communication, and aircraft movement deeply, but moving to commercial pilot means being inside the cockpit doing the flying rather than directing it from the ground, which requires earning new certifications and flight hours from scratch.
steps: Earn FAA Commercial Pilot Certificate
Log 250 minimum required flight hours
Pass FAA written and checkride exams

careerCluster.title: Aviation Inspector
careerCluster.fit: Your deep knowledge of ATC procedures and airspace standards transfers directly to inspecting ATC facilities, but the job shifts from real-time traffic control to methodical compliance review — and comes with a significant pay cut from around $144k to $85k.
steps: Apply for FAA Aviation Safety Inspector (ATC) positions
Complete FAA inspector initial training at MMAC Oklahoma City
Obtain FAA Order 1800.56 inspector qualification

careerCluster.title: Paving Equipment Operator
careerCluster.fit: Both roles involve coordinating movement flow, watching for hazards, and directing traffic in real time, but paving work is hands-on machine operation outdoors rather than communication and oversight from a control room.
steps: Obtain CDL with heavy equipment endorsement
Complete paving equipment operator apprenticeship (LIUNA or IUOE)
Log hours on asphalt paver and roller equipment

careerCluster.title: Airfield Operations Specialist
careerCluster.fit: An air traffic controller moving to airfield operations is a step down in credential and stress — the safety knowledge and airport environment familiarity carry over, but the role shifts from directing live traffic to inspecting and maintaining the physical airfield.
steps: No FAA certification required for ops role
Complete airport operations certification (AAAE CM or ACE)
Apply airfield inspection and wildlife management experience

title: Computer Programmers
keyDrivers: Computer programmers who primarily write code to specifications are among the most directly threatened by AI, as code generation tools can already produce working software from natural-language descriptions. The role lacks physical requirements, legal protections, or deep relationship-based value, it's defined almost entirely by producing a functional output that AI is rapidly learning to deliver. While experienced programmers who architect solutions and understand complex systems will adapt, the traditional role of translating requirements into code is shrinking fast.

taskData.full: Write, analyze, review, and rewrite programs, using workflow chart and diagram, and applying knowledge of computer capabilities, subject matter, and symbolic logic.
Correct errors by making appropriate changes and rechecking the program to ensure that the desired results are produced.
Perform or direct revision, repair, or expansion of existing programs to increase operating efficiency or adapt to new requirements.
Write, update, and maintain computer programs or software packages to handle specific jobs such as tracking inventory, storing or retrieving data, or controlling other equipment.
Consult with managerial, engineering, and technical personnel to clarify program intent, identify problems, and suggest changes.
Conduct trial runs of programs and software applications to be sure they will produce the desired information and that the instructions are correct.
Compile and write documentation of program development and subsequent revisions, inserting comments in the coded instructions so others can understand the program.
Consult with and assist computer operators or system analysts to define and resolve problems in running computer programs.
Perform systems analysis and programming tasks to maintain and control the use of computer systems software as a systems programmer.
Prepare detailed workflow charts and diagrams that describe input, output, and logical operation, and convert them into a series of instructions coded in a computer language.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The core of this job, writing and maintaining code, already shows 33% automation in real AI usage data across over 14,000 observed conversations. Revising and expanding existing programs runs even higher at 36% automation. GitHub's 2024 research found that developers using Copilot completed tasks 55% faster, which means fewer programmer-hours needed per project [1]. BLS projects computer programmer employment to decline 10% from 2023 to 2033, a loss of roughly 12,400 jobs, as companies consolidate programming work into broader software developer roles and offshore routine coding [2].
risks.statLabel: employment expected to shrink over the next decade [2]

opportunities.body: The strongest augmentation signal sits in consulting with teams to resolve problems in running programs: 77% augmentation rate across 747 conversations. AI acts as a diagnostic partner here, but the human still drives the fix. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey found that 76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools, but only 43% trust the accuracy of the output [3]. That trust gap is where experienced programmers add value: reviewing AI-generated code, catching subtle logic errors, and understanding system context that AI misses. Roles that combine programming skill with domain knowledge, like systems analysis or DevOps, are growing even as pure coding positions shrink.
opportunities.statLabel: projected growth in software developer roles that programmers can transition into [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI code-generation tools (GitHub Copilot, Claude, or similar) on real production tasks now, and track where they fail. The 2024 Stack Overflow survey found 43% of developers don't trust AI output accuracy [3], which means the ability to audit, debug, and correct AI-generated code is immediately valuable. Over the next six months, build depth in areas AI handles worst for this role: cross-team troubleshooting, understanding legacy system context, and translating business requirements into technical specs. Those collaborative, context-heavy tasks show the highest augmentation rates and lowest automation rates in the data.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Pure coding skills alone are a shrinking market. GitHub's research shows AI is already handling a meaningful share of routine code generation [1]. A stronger entry strategy: build a portfolio that demonstrates code review, debugging complex systems, and integrating components rather than writing greenfield code from scratch. Pair programming proficiency with a specific domain (healthcare data, financial systems, embedded hardware) to differentiate from both AI tools and generalist programmers. Consider aiming for software developer or DevOps roles rather than "computer programmer" titles specifically, since BLS projects software developers to grow 17% over the same period programmers decline [2].

quotes.quote: The programmers who are thriving with AI coding tools are the ones who treat the output as a first draft from a junior engineer — always reviewed, often restructured. Speed went up. So did the importance of architecture judgment, because now you are reviewing a lot more code than you used to write.

AI code generation has shifted the bottleneck in programming from writing code to understanding what you wrote. Programmers who can read, debug, and reason about large codebases quickly are more valuable than ever — that skill does not go away when the AI writes the first draft.

The entry bar has shifted from "can you write code" to "can you evaluate code, catch bugs in AI output, and reason about system design." Bootcamp graduates who understand that shift — and build portfolios that demonstrate it — are getting hired. Those who treat AI tools as a substitute for fundamentals are not.

67% of developers report using AI coding tools in their workflow, but the survey shows senior developers are adopting them at higher rates than juniors — not because they need more help, but because they can evaluate the output critically and redirect it when it is wrong.
Developers with AI tools completed tasks 55% faster. The skill isn't prompting, it's evaluating what comes back and knowing when the output is subtly wrong.
76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools in their development process, but only 43% trust the accuracy of AI output.

Employment of software developers is projected to grow 17 percent from 2023 to 2033, while computer programmers are projected to decline 10 percent, suggesting the market rewards broader engineering skills over pure coding.
Developers who pair coding with domain-specific knowledge and system design responsibilities report higher satisfaction and less concern about AI displacement.

careerCluster.title: Information Security Analyst
careerCluster.fit: Your programming background gives you a real head start — you already understand how software works, which makes spotting vulnerabilities easier — but this is a step up that requires learning threat monitoring, security protocols, and compliance frameworks you haven't been responsible for before.
steps: Earn CompTIA Security+ certification
Build a home lab, practice network monitoring
Study NIST cybersecurity framework basics

careerCluster.title: Computer Science Researcher
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug complex programs, but this is a step up into original research, mathematical modeling, and designing the systems themselves rather than implementing specs others define.
steps: Earn a Master's or PhD in Computer Science
Publish or contribute to peer-reviewed research
Build projects applying novel algorithms or models

careerCluster.title: Software Developer
careerCluster.fit: You already write, test, and fix code daily, but Software Developer is a step up — it adds system design, user needs analysis, and ownership of the full software lifecycle beyond just the code itself.
steps: Lead a full project from design to deployment
Study systems design (e.g., Grokking the System Design Interview)
Build 2-3 portfolio projects with documented architecture decisions

careerCluster.title: Data Scientist
careerCluster.fit: Programming experience with data handling and logic carries over directly, but Data Science is a step up that requires statistical modeling, machine learning, and analytical skills that go well beyond writing functional code.
steps: Complete a statistics or ML course (Coursera, edX)
Learn Python data stack: pandas, scikit-learn, matplotlib
Build 2-3 portfolio projects with real datasets

careerCluster.title: Information Technology Project Manager
careerCluster.fit: You already work with technical teams and debug complex systems, but this is a step up — the job shifts from writing code to owning schedules, budgets, and people.
steps: Earn PMP or CAPM certification
Lead an internal project as unofficial PM
Learn Jira or MS Project for scheduling

careerCluster.title: Computer System Architect
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug code, which is real systems knowledge, but this is a step up into designing full systems, directing others, and owning hardware and infrastructure decisions, not just the software.
steps: Earn AWS or Azure Solutions Architect certification
Take on system design ownership in current role
Study networking fundamentals via CompTIA Network+

careerCluster.title: Database Architect
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug code that handles data, but Database Architect is a step up — it requires designing the full data infrastructure that programmers build on top of, not just writing programs that use it.
steps: Earn Oracle or Microsoft database architect certification
Build 2-3 schema design and data modeling portfolio projects
Learn ERwin, ER/Studio, or similar data modeling tools

careerCluster.title: Software Quality Assurance Analyst
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug code and run trial programs, which maps directly to QA work, but this is a step up because the role owns the full testing process, defect documentation, and quality standards rather than just fixing your own code.
steps: Earn ISTQB Foundation Level certification
Learn Selenium, JIRA, or similar bug tracking tools
Build a test plan portfolio with real defect reports

careerCluster.title: Web Designer
careerCluster.fit: You already know how to build and fix software, but this role is a step up — it adds user research, visual design, and business strategy on top of the technical work you're used to.
steps: Complete a UX/UI design certification (Google or Nielsen Norman)
Build 3 portfolio projects with Figma wireframes and user testing
Learn SEO fundamentals and e-commerce strategy basics

careerCluster.title: Web Administrator
careerCluster.fit: You already write and debug code, which helps with diagnosing server and web page problems, but Web Administrator is a step up — it adds system security monitoring, disaster recovery, and site infrastructure ownership that programming alone doesn't cover.
steps: Earn CompTIA Security+ or Network+ certification
Set up and manage an Apache or Nginx server hands-on
Learn server log analysis with tools like Splunk or AWStats

careerCluster.title: Web Developer
careerCluster.fit: Computer programmers already write, test, and fix code, so the core skill carries over, but web development is a step up that adds browser compatibility, web-specific frameworks, and user-facing design responsibilities.
steps: Build a web-focused portfolio with 2-3 projects
Learn a frontend framework like React or Vue
Learn a backend framework like Node.js or Django

emergingCareers.title: AI Integration Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Designs and implements pipelines that connect large language models and AI APIs into existing enterprise software systems, ensuring reliability, security, and scalability. Day-to-day work involves prompt engineering, API orchestration, and building middleware layers between AI services and business applications.
emergingCareers.core_tools: LangChain, OpenAI API, Azure OpenAI Service

stat.text: Job postings requiring generative AI skills grew by over 1,000% between 2022 and 2024, with AI integration and deployment roles among the fastest-growing technical positions.
stat.sourceTitle: Future of Work Report: AI at Work

emergingCareers.fit: Your coding and systems-thinking skills transfer directly, but you must shift from writing deterministic logic to orchestrating unpredictable, probabilistic AI outputs.
steps: Complete DeepLearning.AI's LangChain for LLM Application Development course
Build a working middleware project connecting OpenAI API to a mock enterprise database
Earn the Microsoft Azure AI Engineer Associate (AI-102) certification

emergingCareers.title: MLOps Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Builds and maintains the infrastructure and CI/CD pipelines required to deploy, monitor, and retrain machine learning models in production environments. Responsibilities include model versioning, performance monitoring, drift detection, and automating model lifecycle management.

stat.text: MLOps Engineer was among the top 10 fastest-growing job titles on LinkedIn in 2024, with demand concentrated at companies moving from AI experimentation to production deployment at scale.

emergingCareers.fit: Your scripting, automation, and deployment skills transfer directly, but you must now learn to treat trained models as versioned artifacts that degrade over time and need monitoring.
steps: Complete the MLflow Model Registry tutorial and track a scikit-learn model end to end
Deploy a containerized model on AWS SageMaker using a free-tier account
Earn the AWS Certified Machine Learning – Specialty certification

emergingCareers.title: Prompt Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Develops, tests, and iterates on structured prompts and prompt frameworks to optimize the performance of large language models for specific business use cases such as customer support, code generation, or content creation. Works closely with product and engineering teams to translate requirements into reliable AI behaviors.
emergingCareers.core_tools: OpenAI Playground, Anthropic Claude API, PromptFlow

stat.text: Prompt engineering was among the top 5 fastest-growing job skills listed on LinkedIn in 2024, with demand concentrated at companies moving AI from experimentation to production deployment.
stat.sourceTitle: Future of Work Report: AI at Work

emergingCareers.fit: Your debugging mindset and comfort with iterative testing carry over directly, but you must shift from writing instructions for machines to writing instructions for language models, which behave probabilistically rather than deterministically.
steps: Complete DeepLearning.AI's free 'ChatGPT Prompt Engineering for Developers' course
Build three working prompts in OpenAI Playground for code review, bug explanation, and test generation
Publish a GitHub repo documenting your prompt experiments with version history and output comparisons

emergingCareers.title: AI Safety Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Evaluates and mitigates risks in AI systems including bias, hallucination, adversarial vulnerabilities, and misuse, implementing technical safeguards and red-teaming protocols before and after model deployment. Works within trust-and-safety or responsible AI teams to ensure models meet regulatory and ethical standards.
emergingCareers.core_tools: Garak, Microsoft Azure AI Content Safety, Weights & Biases

stat.text: The EU AI Act, which entered into force in August 2024, mandates conformity assessments and technical safety documentation for high-risk AI systems, directly driving demand for AI safety and compliance engineering roles across organizations operating in or selling into the EU.
stat.sourceTitle: EU AI Act: first regulation on artificial intelligence

emergingCareers.fit: Your coding skills transfer directly, but you must shift from building features to actively breaking and auditing AI systems for harm.
steps: Complete DeepLearning.AI's 'Red Teaming LLMs' short course
Run Garak against an open-source LLM and document findings in a public GitHub repo
Earn the Microsoft Azure AI Fundamentals (AI-900) certification with focus on responsible AI modules

title: Software Developers
keyDrivers: Software developers are in a paradoxical position: AI code generation tools are already transforming the work, compressing routine coding tasks dramatically, yet developers who can architect systems, understand business needs, and manage AI-generated code will be more productive than ever. The role lacks physical presence or legal accountability protections, making pure coding work highly vulnerable. The strongest protection is that demand for software keeps growing, and experienced developers are well-positioned to direct and validate AI coding tools, but fewer developers may be needed to produce the same output.

taskData.full: Monitor functioning of equipment to ensure system operates in conformance with specifications.
Modify existing software to correct errors, adapt it to new hardware, or upgrade interfaces and improve performance.
Analyze user needs and software requirements to determine feasibility of design within time and cost constraints.
Develop or direct software system testing or validation procedures, programming, or documentation.
Confer with systems analysts, engineers, programmers and others to design systems and to obtain information on project limitations and capabilities, performance requirements and interfaces.
Store, retrieve, and manipulate data for analysis of system capabilities and requirements.
Supervise the work of programmers, technologists and technicians and other engineering and scientific personnel.
Design, develop and modify software systems, using scientific analysis and mathematical models to predict and measure outcomes and consequences of design.
Prepare reports or correspondence concerning project specifications, activities, or status.
Determine system performance standards.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Data storage and retrieval work - the kind of database querying, data wrangling, and scripting that junior developers spend significant time on - shows 42.6% automation in over 2, 000 observed conversations, the clearest signal of AI handling real execution work in this role [1]. GitHub Copilot and similar tools are now used by 76% of developers, and entry-level hiring has contracted as AI handles more of the code-to-spec work that once required junior headcount [3].
risks.statLabel: of developers now use or plan to use AI coding tools [3]

opportunities.body: Analyzing user needs and software requirements - the most important task with AI data - shows 44% augmentation across 4, 643 conversations, a strong signal that system design and requirements work keeps humans in the loop even as AI assists [1]. This role scores 4 out of 5 on both specialized expertise and directing AI tools, reflecting that the most valuable developer work involves architectural judgment and cross-system thinking that AI augments rather than replaces [2]. BLS projects 17% employment growth through 2034 - 40, 000+ annual openings - concentrated in roles requiring full-system ownership.
opportunities.statLabel: of developers spend more time reviewing AI code than they save generating it [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate move is to stop writing boilerplate code manually and start spending that time on the design decisions AI cannot make - system architecture, API contract design, cross-team requirements gathering. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas that show high augmentation rather than automation: requirements analysis, system design tradeoffs, and the stakeholder conversations that determine what gets built [1]. Developers who own the full system - requirements through deployment through operations - are more durable than those who specialize in execution [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The portfolio that gets hired in 2026 shows system-level thinking, not just clean code: deploy a full application with a database, API, and frontend, document the architectural decisions, and explain why you made them. Entry-level coding tasks are under the most direct AI pressure, so demonstrating judgment that goes beyond code output is how new developers differentiate [3]. BLS projects strong growth but the market has shifted - generic web development skills are less valuable than domain-specific technical depth or expertise in AI-assisted development workflows [2].

quotes.quote: 76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools in their development process — but the differentiated skill is knowing when to trust the output and when to override it. Learning to prompt for specific architectural patterns and then critically review what comes back is the new core loop.

The developers I see thriving with AI coding tools are the ones who treat the output as a first draft from a junior engineer — always reviewed, often restructured. Speed went up. So did the importance of architecture judgment, because now you're reviewing a lot more code than you used to write.

The entry bar has shifted from 'can you write code' to 'can you evaluate code, catch bugs in AI output, and reason about system design.' Bootcamp graduates who understand that shift — and build portfolios that demonstrate it — are getting hired. Those who treat AI tools as a substitute for fundamentals are not.
quotes.attribution: ThoughtWorks Technology Radar, 'The Changing Shape of Software Entry Roles'

quotes.quote: Only 43% of developers who use AI coding tools say they trust the accuracy of the output most of the time. A portfolio that demonstrates debugging AI-generated code, catching subtle logic errors, and writing comprehensive tests for AI-assisted features signals exactly the judgment gap employers are trying to hire for.

title: Information Security Analysts
jobTitles: Information Technology Security Analyst (IT Security Analyst)
keyDrivers: Security analysts face an ever-evolving landscape of threats where attackers constantly find novel ways to breach systems, making genuine human judgment in ambiguous, high-stakes situations essential. They spend enormous amounts of time on alert triage and log review that AI will compress, letting them focus on the harder investigative and strategic work. As AI expands the digital attack surface and creates new threat vectors, demand for skilled humans who can direct and validate AI-powered security tools will grow substantially.

taskData.full: Monitor current reports of computer viruses to determine when to update virus protection systems.
Develop plans to safeguard computer files against accidental or unauthorized modification, destruction, or disclosure and to meet emergency data processing needs.
Modify computer security files to incorporate new software, correct errors, or change individual access status.
Encrypt data transmissions and erect firewalls to conceal confidential information as it is being transmitted and to keep out tainted digital transfers.
Monitor use of data files and regulate access to safeguard information in computer files.
Confer with users to discuss issues such as computer data access needs, security violations, and programming changes.
Perform risk assessments and execute tests of data processing system to ensure functioning of data processing activities and security measures.
Review violations of computer security procedures and discuss procedures with violators to ensure violations are not repeated.
Document computer security and emergency measures policies, procedures, and tests.
Coordinate implementation of computer system plan with establishment personnel and outside vendors.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Security policy documentation shows 41.2% automation in observed AI usage - the most direct signal that AI is handling a real slice of the written output work in this role [1]. The bigger structural risk is at the entry level: AI-assisted SIEM platforms and automated triage tools are absorbing tier-1 alert monitoring, which has historically been the on-ramp into the field [2].
risks.statLabel: unfilled cybersecurity jobs globally in 2024 [4]

opportunities.body: Risk assessment and encryption work shows strong augmentation patterns - 47.9% and 58.7% respectively - with thousands of observed conversations where analysts stay firmly in the loop [1]. The role scores a 5 out of 5 on directing AI tools, and every security breach carries regulatory and legal consequences that organizations cannot delegate to automated systems [2]. BLS projects 28% employment growth through 2034, driven by demand for analysts who can interpret AI-flagged threats and respond to novel attack patterns.
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings, driven by expanding attack surfaces and regulatory requirements [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start logging time against AI-assisted detection tools - specifically learning to configure and tune SIEM alert thresholds rather than just monitoring outputs. Over the next six months, build depth in incident response and threat hunting, the work that sits above automated triage and requires judgment under real operational pressure [2]. Analysts who can direct AI tools and own the investigation layer are pulling ahead of those whose value was in manual alert review [1].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The entry bar has shifted: Security+ certification plus hands-on lab work on TryHackMe or Hack The Box now signals more than a CS degree alone, because hiring managers want evidence of practical investigation skills, not just coursework. Build a portfolio that shows you can operate in realistic attack scenarios - document a mock incident response from detection through remediation. The talent shortage is real and the growth projections are among the strongest in tech, but the jobs available increasingly require intermediate skills, not starter-level monitoring [2].

quotes.quote: AI-powered SIEM tools have raised the floor on detection — but they've also raised the volume of alerts analysts have to triage. The skills that matter now are knowing what to investigate and what to suppress, and that requires deep familiarity with your environment, not just the tooling.

The rule generation and anomaly detection layers are getting automated quickly. What stays human is the threat modeling — understanding the attacker's logic well enough to find what the automated systems aren't tuned to catch. That's where security professionals need to invest their development time.
quotes.attribution: ACM Queue, 'Automation and Human Judgment in Security Operations'

quotes.quote: Security+ gets you in the door, but hiring managers now want to see lab work — TryHackMe, Hack The Box, a home lab with a SIEM. Candidates who can show they've done the hands-on work, not just passed the exam, are moving through our pipeline significantly faster.

CISA's AI roadmap identifies the ability to assess AI-enabled threats, secure AI systems, and use AI tools for cyber defense as priority competencies for the next generation of security professionals. Candidates who can speak to all three sides of that triangle — threat, defense, and tooling — will stand out in a field where AI literacy is becoming a baseline expectation.

title: Computer and Information Systems Managers
keyDrivers: IT managers succeed because they deeply understand their organization's people, systems, and strategic priorities, context that can't be scraped from a database. Much of their value comes from navigating competing priorities across departments, managing vendor relationships, and making high-stakes technology decisions under uncertainty. As AI transforms every part of business, these managers become the key decision-makers directing how AI systems are adopted, integrated, and governed within their organizations.

taskData.full: Manage backup, security and user help systems.
Provide users with technical support for computer problems.
Assign and review the work of systems analysts, programmers, and other computer-related workers.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: As AI handles more of the technical work managed below this role - automated testing, code generation, infrastructure monitoring - IT department headcounts are shrinking, which can create pressure on management layers above them [2]. Consulting with users on computing needs shows 15.1% automation, meaning some of the upfront requirements work is being handled by AI assistants before it reaches managers [1]. The risk is not direct replacement but scope reduction: fewer technical staff to manage, and AI absorbing some of the coordination work.
risks.statLabel: of CIOs plan to reduce IT headcount through AI-driven automation [4]

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on both interpersonal complexity and managing complex human systems - the decisions that move organizations forward involve trade-offs that require reading people and politics, not just processing data [2]. Consulting and vendor coordination work shows 53.9% augmentation with over 600 observed conversations, suggesting AI acts as a research and prep tool here rather than a replacement [1]. BLS projects 15% employment growth through 2034 with a median salary of $169, 510, making this one of the more durable management tracks in tech.
opportunities.statLabel: of AI initiatives require executive-level coordination to move from pilot to production [4]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The decisions holding up best are the ones requiring organizational judgment - evaluating which AI tools to adopt, managing teams through workflow changes, and owning the security and reliability of systems that run on AI infrastructure. Build fluency with AI-assisted monitoring and planning tools so that evaluating vendor AI claims is grounded in direct experience rather than delegated to others [1]. Over the next six months, develop deeper expertise in AI governance and risk management, which is becoming a board-level concern and a natural extension of this role [2].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: This role typically requires 5 or more years in technical roles before the transition to management, and pathways through software development, security, or systems administration all lead here. The investment pays off - the salary and growth projections are among the strongest in management, and the role's structural protections (interpersonal complexity, accountability, organizational judgment) make it more durable than many of the technical roles below it. Build a track record of owning cross-functional projects and making sound technology investment calls [2].

quotes.quote: The IT leaders who are most effective right now are the ones who can evaluate AI vendor claims critically and translate the organizational tradeoffs to their boards without overselling or underselling. That judgment — which tools to adopt, at what pace, for which use cases — is not delegatable.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'The CIO's Role in AI Governance'

quotes.quote: AI is changing the IT manager's job from managing systems to managing decisions about systems. The managers pulling ahead are building fluency in AI capabilities fast enough to challenge vendor roadmaps, not just implement them.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'State of AI in the Enterprise'

quotes.quote: The fastest path into IT leadership is through roles where you're already coordinating across technical and business stakeholders — tech lead, staff engineer, program manager. The hard part isn't the technical knowledge, it's learning to translate it. Build that in your current role before the title changes.

quotes.quote: AI tool adoption in software teams grew significantly in 2024, with the majority of professional developers now using AI assistants in their workflow. Managers who understand how these tools change team velocity, code review requirements, and quality assurance processes will have a concrete advantage in technical leadership roles.

title: Web Developers
keyDrivers: Web development involves writing code for websites and applications, exactly the kind of well-structured, pattern-based work that AI coding tools are already handling with increasing competence. The role has no physical component, no legal accountability, and delivers output where clients care about results, not who or what built it. As AI code generation continues to improve, much of the standard web development workload can be completed faster and cheaper by AI-assisted workflows, significantly reducing demand for dedicated web developers.

risks.body: AI can already automate core web development tasks like building sites and writing supporting code, especially boilerplate and routine implementations. Entry-level tech hiring dropped 25% year-over-year in 2024, and software developer employment for workers aged 22-25 declined nearly 20% from its 2022 peak [1]. Developers who have not yet built depth in judgment, architecture, or system design are the most exposed.
risks.statLabel: drop in entry-level tech hiring, year-over-year [6]

opportunities.body: 80% of developers now use AI tools, but trust in AI output collapsed from 40% to 29% year-over-year, and 66% say they spend more time fixing AI-generated code than they save writing it [2]. The emerging skill is knowing what to give AI, how to structure the problem, and how to catch if the output comes back wrong. Researchers are calling this context engineering [3].
opportunities.statLabel: of developers spend more time fixing AI code than they save writing it [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate move is to treat AI output like untrusted code: review every diff, question every assumption, document the decisions AI does not make for you [4]. Over the next six months, build depth where AI handles things worst - system design, cross-team architecture decisions, and requirements too ambiguous for a model to interpret reliably [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Companies now expect AI literacy from day one - build a GitHub portfolio of real projects that shows you can direct AI tools, review their output, and ship working systems [5]. Code review discipline and catching AI failures matter more to hiring managers than volume of code written.

quotes.quote: Developers are ready to push back on enterprise AI through a nuanced conversation about trust, reliability, and the enduring value of human expertise.
Code review is a conversation, not a test.
We're entering an era where interns and junior developers are showing up already fluent in the best tools for AI code-gen on the market.
Being good isn't good enough.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

title: Web and Digital Interface Designers
keyDrivers: Web and digital interface design is one of the fields most directly disrupted by AI, tools can already generate layouts, prototypes, and even production-ready code from simple prompts. The work is fully remote, requires no licensing, and while human taste and user empathy still matter, clients are increasingly comfortable with AI-assisted or AI-generated design. Without strong legal protections or physical presence requirements, the typical practitioner in this role faces significant displacement pressure as AI design tools continue to improve rapidly.

taskData.full: Collaborate with management or users to develop e-commerce strategies and to integrate these strategies with Web sites.
Collaborate with web development professionals, such as front-end or back-end developers, to complete the full scope of Web development projects.
Communicate with network personnel or Web site hosting agencies to address hardware or software issues affecting Web sites.
Conduct user research to determine design requirements and analyze user feedback to improve design quality.
Confer with management or development teams to prioritize needs, resolve conflicts, develop content criteria, or choose solutions.
Create searchable indices for Web page content.
Create Web models or prototypes that include physical, interface, logical, or data models.
Design, build, or maintain Web sites, using authoring or scripting languages, content creation tools, management tools, and digital media.
Develop and document style guidelines for Web site content.
Develop new visual design concepts and modify concepts based on stakeholder feedback.

risks.body: AEI task data shows that core deliverables for this role - building and maintaining websites and developing style guidelines - face automation rates of 41-45%, meaning a meaningful share of routine production work can now be generated without a designer in the loop [1]. Generative design tools are compressing the time required to produce initial wireframes, style sheets, and basic UI components, which tends to reduce the number of junior and mid-level seats needed per project. BLS projects only 9,100 annual openings against a field that has grown quickly, leaving the entry-level job market tighter than the headline +8% growth rate suggests [2].
risks.statLabel: drop in entry-level web design job postings year-over-year [4]

opportunities.body: The strongest durability signal for this role is collaboration: tasks involving working with management on strategy and coordinating with development teams show augmentation rates above 36%, meaning AI assists rather than replaces the judgment calls that happen at the intersection of business goals and user needs [1]. Designers who own the brief - conducting user research, interpreting stakeholder feedback, and setting accessibility standards - are doing work that requires contextual reasoning and accountability that AI tools still handle poorly. The Stack Overflow 2024 Developer Survey found that 63% of developers cite 'AI tools lack context of codebase' as a key obstacle, a gap that a designer with deep product knowledge can fill [3].
opportunities.statLabel: of developers cite design-to-dev handoff as their top friction point [5]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The immediate priority is shifting time toward the tasks AI cannot compress: user research sessions, accessibility audits, and cross-functional facilitation. Learning to use AI generation tools (Figma AI, Midjourney, v0) as a starting point rather than a finishing line - then critically editing and validating output against real user data - is the difference between being replaced by the tools and directing them. Over six months, building depth in design systems governance and component documentation creates a track record that is harder to replicate with prompts alone. Roles that bridge design and engineering, such as design technologist or product designer with front-end fluency, are holding up better in hiring than pure visual production roles.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: A portfolio that leads with the research and decision-making behind the designs carries more weight than one that showcases polished visuals alone, because hiring managers can now generate polished visuals cheaply. Concrete credentials worth pursuing include a Nielsen Norman Group UX Certificate, a Google UX Design Certificate (for foundational credibility), or a dedicated course in accessibility (WCAG 2.2 auditing, axe tools). Building in public - writing a case study that documents how user research shaped a specific design decision - demonstrates the kind of judgment that differentiates a mid-level candidate in a compressed market. Targeting industries with high regulatory or compliance requirements (healthcare, fintech, government) tends to insulate design roles from the fastest commoditization pressure.

quotes.quote: 76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools in their workflow — for web designers, that means AI is generating layout scaffolding, color palettes, and copy at scale. The irreplaceable work is the user research and stakeholder facilitation that tells you whether the AI-generated output is actually solving the right problem.

Generative AI has automated a meaningful share of visual production in web design. The designers who are thriving moved upstream into user research and design systems — work that requires organizational context and user empathy that tools can't replicate. The production layer is not where you want to be building your career right now.

Only 43% of developers say they trust AI output accuracy most of the time. For design portfolios, this means case studies that document the gap between initial AI-generated concepts and final shipped designs — showing how user feedback, accessibility constraints, and brand guidelines shaped the outcome — carry more weight than polished visuals alone.

Accessibility and UX research are the growth areas in web design right now — and they require skills that can't be quickly automated. Designers entering the field who build fluency in WCAG standards and usability testing methods are differentiating themselves from those who compete primarily on visual execution speed.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

title: Web Administrators
keyDrivers: Web administration involves managing servers, configurations, security patches, and uptime, tasks that are increasingly automated by cloud platforms and AI-driven infrastructure tools. The role requires no physical presence, no licensure, and limited novel judgment, leaving few natural defenses against automation. While organizational knowledge provides some buffer, the core tasks are well-defined and pattern-based, making this one of the more vulnerable technology roles.

taskData.full: Monitor systems for intrusions or denial of service attacks, and report security breaches to appropriate personnel.
Determine sources of Web page or server problems, and take action to correct such problems.
Correct testing-identified problems, or recommend actions for their resolution.
Review or update Web page content or links in a timely manner, using appropriate tools.
Back up or modify applications and related data to provide for disaster recovery.
Track, compile, and analyze Web site usage data.
Document application and Web site changes or change procedures.
Collaborate with Web developers to create and operate internal and external Web sites, or to manage projects, such as e-marketing campaigns.
Collaborate with development teams to discuss, analyze, or resolve usability issues.
Gather, analyze, or document user feedback to locate or resolve sources of problems.

risks.body: The most important tasks in web administration are already showing significant AI involvement. Reviewing and updating web content, a core daily task, has a 46% automation rate in AI conversations, meaning nearly half the time AI handles it end-to-end without human input [1]. Troubleshooting server problems shows a lower 13% automation rate but a combined 38% AI involvement, and with AI coding assistants now used by 76% of developers, the tools that automate routine web maintenance are becoming standard issue [2]. The World Economic Forum's 2025 Future of Jobs Report projects that roles focused on digital access provisioning, which includes web administration, are among the fastest-declining job categories globally through 2030 [3].
risks.statLabel: of web admin tasks now handled by cloud platform automation [4]

opportunities.body: Security monitoring, the single most important task in this role by importance, shows a 27% augmentation rate alongside its 29% automation rate, meaning AI assists more often than it replaces the human on threat detection [1]. That pattern makes sense: deciding whether an anomaly is a real breach and coordinating the response requires judgment and organizational context that AI tools flag but can't act on alone. The 2025 WEF report identifies cybersecurity specialists as one of the fastest-growing roles globally, and web administrators with security expertise sit at the intersection of that demand [3]. Web admins who build depth in incident response, access control policy, and compliance auditing are doing work that stays human-in-the-loop because the consequences of getting it wrong are high.
opportunities.statLabel: average time to fill a cybersecurity role, signaling persistent demand for security-adjacent skills [5]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-assisted monitoring and content management tools now, not to replace what you do, but to learn where they fail. The 76% of developers already using AI coding assistants in 2024 aren't just faster; they're learning the boundaries of what the tools can and can't handle [2]. Over the next six months, build toward security specialization: incident response, vulnerability assessment, and compliance frameworks like SOC 2 or ISO 27001. These are the areas where the data shows AI augments rather than automates, and where organizational accountability keeps a human in the decision seat [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: A pure web administration role is shrinking. The stronger entry point is web administration combined with cybersecurity, which the WEF identifies as a top-growth field through 2030 [3]. Build a portfolio that shows both: a well-maintained site plus a write-up of how you secured it, including access controls, SSL configuration, and monitoring setup. Certifications like CompTIA Security+ or AWS Certified Cloud Practitioner signal practical skill more than a general IT degree for this path.

emergingCareers.fit: Your experience managing servers and keeping web infrastructure running translates well, but you need to shift from reactive manual fixes to writing automated security checks that run inside code deployment pipelines.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

title: Software QA Analyst
keyDrivers: QA testing is heavily pattern-based work, writing test cases, running regression suites, and identifying bugs, that AI tools are already automating at increasing speed and accuracy. The role has no physical presence requirements, no licensing protections, and limited need for novel judgment, leaving few hard barriers against displacement. While some QA professionals will shift toward managing automated testing pipelines and evaluating AI-generated code, the overall number of positions is likely to shrink significantly as AI handles the bulk of testing work.

taskData.full: Identify, analyze, and document problems with program function, output, online screen, or content.
Document software defects, using a bug tracking system, and report defects to software developers.
Install, maintain, or use software testing programs.
Document test procedures to ensure replicability and compliance with standards.
Develop testing programs that address areas such as database impacts, software scenarios, regression testing, negative testing, error or bug retests, or usability.
Provide feedback and recommendations to developers on software usability and functionality.
Create or maintain databases of known test defects.
Monitor program performance to ensure efficient and problem-free operations.
Monitor bug resolution efforts and track successes.
Design test plans, scenarios, scripts, or procedures.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI code generation tools are writing tests automatically as part of development workflows, compressing the manual test writing work that has traditionally anchored this role [3]. Bug documentation - the most important task with AI data - shows 21.8% automation across 1, 332 conversations, suggesting AI is handling some of the routine defect reporting and ticket creation [1]. This role has the lowest resilience score in the software career cluster, reflecting limited structural protections: most QA work lacks physical presence requirements, real-time judgment demands, or legal accountability that would insulate it from automation pressure.
risks.statLabel: of developers say AI tools improved their bug detection [3]

opportunities.body: Providing feedback and recommendations to developers shows 66.6% augmentation across 4, 148 conversations - the strongest augmentation signal in this role, indicating AI acts as a research and analysis tool while humans own the quality judgment [1]. The work that holds up best is quality architecture: defining test strategies, evaluating system-wide risk, and communicating findings to engineering and product teams. Roles shifting toward quality engineering - writing test frameworks, building CI/CD pipelines, evaluating AI-generated code for correctness - are where QA skills are growing in value rather than declining [3].
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings for QA analysts, concentrated in roles bridging testing and development [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start moving from executing test cases to designing test strategy - the architectural decisions about what to test, how to structure automation frameworks, and how to evaluate AI-generated code are where human judgment matters most. Over the next six months, build skills in test automation engineering (Playwright, pytest, or Cypress) and CI/CD pipeline integration, since the QA roles growing in value are those that own the quality infrastructure rather than run scripts against it [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Manual QA testing as a standalone role is under meaningful pressure from AI code generation and automated testing tools, but quality engineering - the discipline of designing systems that catch bugs by construction - is growing in demand [3]. Build a portfolio that shows test framework design and automation engineering, not just test case execution. A background in software development alongside QA skills is increasingly the combination that leads to senior roles [2].

quotes.quote: AI is writing a lot more code now — which means QA engineers are being asked to review a lot more output that looks right but may not be. The highest-value skill shift is toward adversarial thinking: not just does this work, but how does it fail, and what does the model get confidently wrong.
quotes.attribution: ACM Queue, 'Testing in the Age of AI-Generated Code'

quotes.quote: 62% of developers say AI tools have improved their ability to find and fix bugs — but the strategic QA work is shifting toward designing the test architecture that catches what AI misses. Building skills in exploratory testing, security testing, and API contract testing positions QA engineers above the automation layer rather than beneath it.

The QA roles with the most demand are the ones bridging testing and development — engineers who can write code, understand CI/CD pipelines, and own automation frameworks. Pure manual testing roles are contracting. The transition to SDET-style work is where the career growth is.
quotes.attribution: Lightcast, Software QA Labor Market Report

quotes.quote: AI-generated code increases the surface area that QA engineers need to cover. Engineers who specialize in validating AI outputs — testing for consistency, edge case failures, and hallucinated logic — are in a genuinely new and high-demand niche that traditional QA tooling wasn't built for.

emergingCareers.title: AI QA Engineer
emergingCareers.description: Designs and runs test suites for AI-powered products — evaluating model output quality, testing edge cases in LLM behavior, validating safety guardrails, and building regression frameworks that catch model degradation after updates. Applies QA discipline to probabilistic systems where outputs are never fully deterministic.

stat.text: AI QA is now a distinct engineering discipline with dedicated tooling — companies building LLM products report spending 30-40% of development time on evaluation and testing, creating demand for engineers who specialize in AI-specific quality assurance.

emergingCareers.search_query: "AI QA engineer" OR "LLM testing engineer" jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your systematic thinking about test coverage, edge cases, and regression testing applies directly — the shift is from testing deterministic code paths to testing probabilistic outputs, which requires statistical rigor rather than just boolean pass/fail.
steps: Build an eval framework using Braintrust or LangSmith for a public LLM app and document 5 failure modes you discovered through systematic testing
Complete Anthropic's free 'Evals' documentation and implement a custom eval suite for a specific domain (customer support, code review, or document Q&A)
Study the HELM benchmark methodology and replicate one evaluation dimension on a model of your choice, publishing your findings publicly

emergingCareers.title: Prompt QA Specialist

stat.text: Prompt injection and jailbreaking were among the top 10 OWASP security risks for LLM applications in 2024, creating demand for specialists who can systematically find and document prompt vulnerabilities before deployment.

emergingCareers.search_query: "prompt QA" OR "prompt engineer" testing evaluation jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your QA instinct for finding edge cases and documenting failure modes is exactly the skill that makes a good prompt red-teamer — the domain shifts from code behavior to language model behavior.
steps: Work through the OWASP LLM Top 10 and attempt each attack type against a publicly accessible LLM app, documenting successful exploits and mitigations
Run Garak against an open-source model and write a structured test report covering injection, jailbreak, and data leakage categories
Build a prompt regression test suite using OpenAI Evals or PromptFlow that detects behavioral changes across model versions

emergingCareers.search_query: "LLM evaluation engineer" OR "AI eval engineer" jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your experience writing test cases and thinking about coverage maps directly to eval design — the new skill is building scoring metrics for outputs that don't have a single correct answer.

emergingCareers.search_query: "test automation engineer" AI-powered OR "AI testing" jobs 2025
emergingCareers.fit: Your existing automation skills are the foundation — the shift is from writing every test manually to directing AI tools to generate, maintain, and prioritize tests while you focus on strategy and edge case design.
