Implement self-healing test locators using Applitools or Heal.dev and document the reduction in test maintenance overhead
Build an LLM-powered test case generator that takes a feature description and outputs Playwright tests, with a QA step to filter out hallucinated assertions

title: Computer and Information Research Scientists
keyDrivers: These scientists work at the frontier of computing, tackling genuinely novel problems where pattern-matching from existing solutions fails, the kind of deep, creative reasoning that AI struggles to replicate. They are also the people most likely to be designing, directing, and evaluating AI systems themselves, making their expertise more valuable as AI expands. However, the role lacks physical or legal protections, meaning its resilience depends entirely on staying ahead of the technology curve.

taskData.full: Analyze problems to develop solutions involving computer hardware and software.
Apply theoretical expertise and innovation to create or apply new technology, such as adapting principles for applying computers to new uses.
Assign or schedule tasks to meet work priorities and goals.
Design computers and the software that runs them.
Conduct logical analyses of business, scientific, engineering, and other technical problems, formulating mathematical models of problems for solution by computers.
Meet with managers, vendors, and others to solicit cooperation and resolve problems.
Maintain network hardware and software, direct network security measures, and monitor networks to ensure availability to system users.
Participate in multidisciplinary projects in areas such as virtual reality, human-computer interaction, or robotics.
Evaluate project plans and proposals to assess feasibility issues.
Direct daily operations of departments, coordinating project activities with other departments.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Weighted automation across observed tasks runs at 38.9% - meaningful signal that AI is handling portions of the code writing, data analysis, and model evaluation work that researchers do alongside their primary investigations [1]. The risk is concentrated at the entry level: research assistants and junior scientists who spend the majority of their time on data processing and literature review face the most direct competition from AI tools that can handle those tasks. BLS projects 20% employment growth but notes that most opportunities will favor those with doctoral degrees and demonstrated research output [2].
risks.statLabel: AI research papers now list an LLM as co-author or contributor [3]

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on novel judgment - the highest possible rating - reflecting that original scientific contribution, hypothesis generation, and working at knowledge frontiers are structurally resistant to automation [2]. Mathematical modeling and analysis shows 38.6% augmentation even as automation rises, indicating AI works as a research accelerator in the core work rather than displacing it entirely [1]. A10 scores a 5, meaning this role is well-positioned to direct and benefit from AI tools as they improve - researchers who use AI to run more experiments and test more hypotheses faster are producing more output with the same effort.
opportunities.statLabel: more AI research papers published in 2024 vs. 2020, expanding demand for original researchers [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The research work most protected from displacement is the part that requires genuine originality - identifying novel problems, designing experiments that test real unknowns, and interpreting ambiguous results in context of the field. Start using AI tools to accelerate the literature review and initial modeling phases, which frees more time for the creative and judgment-intensive work at the frontier [1]. Over the next year, build depth in AI research itself or in domain-specific applications of machine learning, since those intersections carry both higher demand and stronger protections than generalist computer science research.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Doctoral degrees are the standard credential for this field, and most positions are in universities, government labs, and the research divisions of large technology companies [2]. The path is long but the role's structural protections are strong - original research output is among the hardest things for AI to generate reliably, and the judgment required to identify what is worth investigating is a persistent human advantage. Build a research portfolio that shows systems-level thinking and the ability to publish findings, not just implement existing methods.

quotes.quote: The research tasks where AI earns its keep are computation, literature synthesis, and pattern detection. What it can't do is identify which question is worth asking, or exercise the scientific skepticism to know when a result is too clean. That judgment is still entirely human — and it's the most professionally valuable part of the job.
quotes.attribution: Communications of the ACM, 'AI as a Research Collaborator'

quotes.quote: The researchers I know who are using AI tools most effectively are treating them like powerful but unreliable collaborators. You have to maintain enough command of the domain to know when the output is wrong — and the people who've let those muscles atrophy are the ones getting into trouble.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'Human Oversight in AI-Assisted Research'

quotes.quote: Private sector research roles at AI labs and cloud platforms now account for a significant share of PhD-level hiring in CS. The distinguishing factor is not just technical depth but the ability to identify tractable research problems within applied constraints. Academic training develops the former — the latter often has to be built on the job.
quotes.attribution: Communications of the ACM, 'Career Pathways in AI Research'

quotes.quote: Publication record matters — but what hiring managers at research labs are increasingly screening for is reproducibility and methodological rigor, not just novel results. The candidates who stand out show their work: open code, documented experiments, and honest ablation studies.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'What Research Labs Look For in New Hires'

emergingCareers.title: AI Safety Researcher
emergingCareers.description: Studies failure modes, alignment properties, and societal risks of large AI systems. Designs and runs experiments on model behavior under distribution shift, adversarial prompting, and reward misspecification. Publishes findings that inform how labs build safer, more predictable AI systems.

stat.text: AI safety roles at frontier labs grew over 200% year-over-year in 2024, driven by regulatory pressure and internal safety commitments — with Anthropic, OpenAI, Google DeepMind, and Meta all expanding dedicated safety teams.

emergingCareers.fit: Your research methodology and comfort with formal models of computation transfer directly — alignment work is applied math and empirical science, and your background in CS theory gives you stronger foundations than most safety candidates.

emergingCareers.search_query: "ML research engineer" OR "machine learning engineer" research jobs 2025

title: Data Scientists
keyDrivers: Data scientists face a double-edged situation: AI tools are rapidly automating the core technical tasks of building models, cleaning data, and running analyses that define much of this work today. However, the role is evolving toward directing and validating AI systems, translating business problems into the right technical approach, and interpreting results for decision-makers, skills that keep experienced practitioners valuable. The lack of any licensing requirement or physical presence means the purely technical side of the job is highly exposed, and the role's long-term resilience depends on shifting toward strategic AI oversight rather than hands-on model building.

taskData.full: Analyze, manipulate, or process large sets of data using statistical software.
Apply feature selection algorithms to models predicting outcomes of interest, such as sales, attrition, and healthcare use.
Apply sampling techniques to determine groups to be surveyed or use complete enumeration methods.
Clean and manipulate raw data using statistical software.
Compare models using statistical performance metrics, such as loss functions or proportion of explained variance.
Create graphs, charts, or other visualizations to convey the results of data analysis using specialized software.
Deliver oral or written presentations of the results of mathematical modeling and data analysis to management or other end users.
Design surveys, opinion polls, or other instruments to collect data.
Identify business problems or management objectives that can be addressed through data analysis.
Identify relationships and trends or any factors that could affect the results of research.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Exploratory data analysis, feature engineering, and initial model selection - work that junior data scientists and analysts spend significant time on - are tasks that AI tools now handle reliably and quickly [2]. AutoML platforms and AI-assisted analysis tools are automating the mechanical parts of the data science workflow, which concentrates demand at the senior end of the role where domain expertise and business judgment drive the work. BLS projects 34% employment growth, but hiring patterns suggest that demand is shifting toward data scientists who can translate complex model outputs into organizational decisions, not those who optimize model parameters.
risks.statLabel: of data prep and feature engineering now handled by AutoML tools [4]

opportunities.body: This role scores a 5 out of 5 on directing AI tools - the highest rating - reflecting that data scientists who use AI to run faster experiments, test more hypotheses, and explore larger datasets are producing more output than those doing the same work manually [2]. Domain expertise in healthcare, finance, climate science, or other specialized fields is what differentiates senior data scientists from AI tools and from each other. BLS projects 34% employment growth through 2034, among the strongest projections for any occupation, driven by organizations needing people who can interpret data model outputs and make consequential decisions based on them.
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings, among the highest of any STEM occupation [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The parts of this role most resistant to displacement are problem formulation and decision translation - identifying which questions are worth asking with data, and communicating what model outputs actually mean for business or research decisions. Build depth in a specific domain (healthcare, finance, climate, operations) so that your value comes from understanding the context of the data, not just the mechanics of the analysis [2]. Over the next six months, get comfortable using AI tools to accelerate the EDA and preprocessing work, which frees more time for the judgment-intensive interpretation and stakeholder communication that AI does not handle well.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Most entry-level data scientist positions require at minimum a bachelor's degree in statistics, math, or computer science, and graduate degrees are common for research and senior roles [2]. The portfolio that gets hired shows end-to-end project work: data cleaning, modeling, and a clear explanation of what the findings mean for a real problem. Domain knowledge matters more as AI tools commoditize general-purpose data analysis - specialize in a field where you understand the data's context, not just its structure.

quotes.quote: The data scientists with the most organizational impact are the ones who can translate model outputs into business decisions — not just build the model. As AutoML handles more of the technical execution, the premium on communication and problem framing is only going up.
quotes.attribution: Harvard Business Review, 'The Business Case for Analytical Translators'

quotes.quote: Problem formulation is the highest-leverage skill in data science and the one least touched by AI tools. Identifying which business question is actually worth answering — and what a good answer would look like — still requires deep domain knowledge and organizational context that you can't prompt your way around.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'The Most Important Skill in Data Science'

quotes.quote: Data science is one of the fastest-growing occupations in the labor market — and the demand is no longer concentrated in tech. Healthcare, finance, retail, and logistics are hiring aggressively for people who can work with messy real-world data and connect it to operational decisions. Domain expertise is as valuable as technical skill at the entry level.
quotes.attribution: Lightcast, Data Science Labor Market Outlook

quotes.quote: The most-used data science tools in industry are Python, SQL, and Jupyter notebooks — and 76% of developers are now integrating AI assistants into their workflow. Candidates who can demonstrate working with AI-assisted data pipelines, evaluate model outputs critically, and document their methodology rigorously will stand out as AI tools raise baseline productivity expectations.

title: IT Project Manager
jobTitles: Information Systems Project Manager (IS Project Manager)
IT Program Manager (Information Technology Program Manager)
IT Project Manager (Information Technology Project Manager)
keyDrivers: IT project managers succeed primarily through navigating complex stakeholder relationships, managing competing priorities, and keeping diverse teams aligned, skills that are deeply human and context-dependent. Their value comes from understanding specific organizational dynamics, politics, and histories that AI cannot observe. While AI will automate much of their scheduling, reporting, and status tracking, the lack of physical presence requirements and legal accountability means the role has some vulnerability as AI project management tools grow more capable.

taskData.full: Confer with project personnel to identify and resolve problems.
Schedule and facilitate meetings related to information technology projects.
Manage project execution to ensure adherence to budget, schedule, and scope.
Direct or coordinate activities of project personnel.
Monitor or track project milestones and deliverables.
Initiate, review, or approve modifications to project plans.

Develop or update project plans for information technology projects including information such as project objectives, technologies, systems, information specifications, schedules, funding, and staffing.
Assess current or future customer needs and priorities by communicating directly with customers, conducting surveys, or other methods.
Prepare project status reports by collecting, analyzing, and summarizing information and trends.
Monitor the performance of project team members, providing and documenting performance feedback.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Automation on the observed tasks runs at 34.7%, with AI tools like Notion AI, Jira automation, and AI-assisted reporting handling status updates, meeting notes, and routine project documentation [1]. These tasks are real parts of a project manager's week even if they sit below the most important coordination work, and their automation compresses the hours spent on admin. The structural risk is not displacement of the role but commoditization of the parts that were historically junior or coordinator-level entry points [2].
risks.statLabel: of project status reporting now auto-generated by AI tools [4]

opportunities.body: This role scores a 4 out of 5 on both human relations complexity and managing complex human systems - the work of aligning stakeholders, managing competing priorities, and making judgment calls when projects go sideways is structurally hard to automate [2]. The core value of IT project management sits in the judgment layer: knowing when to escalate, how to negotiate scope, and which technical risks matter to business outcomes - none of which appears in observed AI usage data. As AI-assisted development teams move faster, demand is growing for project managers who can coordinate across AI-augmented workflows and manage the faster delivery cycles that result [3].
opportunities.statLabel: of IT projects fail due to people and process issues, not technology [5]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Adopt AI-assisted project management tools (Notion AI, Linear, GitHub Projects with automation) to eliminate the documentation and status-reporting overhead, which frees more time for the stakeholder work that AI cannot do. Over the next six months, build depth in managing AI-assisted development teams specifically - understanding the delivery dynamics when developers are using Copilot and agents changes how you estimate, scope, and track progress [3]. PMs who can credibly evaluate AI tooling decisions and translate technical AI risk into business terms are increasingly valuable at the executive level.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Most IT project manager roles require a combination of technical background and demonstrated delivery experience - PMP or CAPM certification signals process discipline, but hiring managers want to see projects actually shipped [2]. The fastest path is through technical roles first: software development, QA, or systems administration all provide the foundational credibility that makes IT PM work legible. Agile and Scrum certifications are table stakes in most software organizations [3].

quotes.quote: The IT project managers navigating AI adoption most effectively are the ones who can evaluate which tools actually improve delivery and which add coordination overhead. That judgment — what to adopt, when, for which teams — is not something a tool can make for you.
quotes.attribution: MIT Sloan Management Review, 'The CIO's Role in AI Governance'

quotes.quote: AI is shifting IT project management toward higher-stakes work: more vendor evaluation, more cross-functional change management, more governance decisions. The administrative coordination layer is automating. What remains requires organizational judgment and trust.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'State of AI in the Enterprise'

quotes.quote: PMP certification is often required or preferred for project manager positions, and can lead to higher salaries.

About 31,300 IT project manager openings are projected annually through the decade — but the profile of candidates who get hired is shifting. Hiring managers want to see experience managing AI implementation projects, vendor evaluations, and cross-functional change management, not just traditional waterfall delivery.

title: Retail Salespersons
keyDrivers: Retail sales is increasingly moving online, and even in-store, self-checkout and AI-powered recommendation tools are reducing the need for human salespeople. The work involves limited judgment, no legal accountability, and customers often prefer browsing independently. Physical presence on the sales floor offers some protection, but it's not enough to offset the steady erosion from e-commerce and automation.

taskData.full: Greet customers and ascertain what each customer wants or needs.
Recommend, select, and help locate or obtain merchandise based on customer needs and desires.
Compute sales prices, total purchases, and receive and process cash or credit payment.
Answer questions regarding the store and its merchandise.
Prepare merchandise for purchase or rental.
Open and close cash registers, performing tasks such as counting money, separating charge slips, coupons, and vouchers, balancing cash drawers, and making deposits.
Bag or package purchases and wrap gifts.
Maintain knowledge of current sales and promotions, policies regarding payment and exchanges, and security practices.
Describe merchandise and explain use, operation, and care of merchandise to customers.
Prepare sales slips or sales contracts.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The product recommendation task, one of the heaviest in the role, already shows a 43% automation rate in AI usage data. AI-powered recommendation engines and conversational shopping assistants are handling a meaningful share of the "help the customer find what they want" workflow, which is the core of retail selling. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 81% of sales teams are either using or experimenting with generative AI, with customer-facing product guidance among the top use cases [1]. Meanwhile, BLS projects retail salesperson employment to decline 2% over the next decade, a loss of roughly 86,000 positions, as self-checkout, e-commerce, and AI chat tools reduce headcount needs [2].
risks.statLabel: of consumer product searches now start online, not in store [4]

opportunities.body: Describing merchandise and explaining product use, operation, and care shows a 43% augmentation rate, the highest in the role. This is the consultative, hands-on side of retail selling: demonstrating a product, troubleshooting fit, explaining compatibility. AI can surface specs and comparisons, but the in-person explanation still relies on a human. NRF's 2024 State of Retailing Online report found that retailers investing in AI-assisted clienteling tools see higher average transaction values, because reps spend less time on inventory lookups and more on consultative selling [3]. Answering store and merchandise questions also shows a 29% augmentation rate, suggesting AI is supplementing product knowledge rather than replacing the interaction.
opportunities.statLabel: of salespeople say AI frees time for selling [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI product-lookup tools (chatbots, internal knowledge bases) to answer routine inventory and spec questions faster, so more floor time goes to hands-on demos and consultative conversations. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst in this role: live product demonstration, fitting and compatibility troubleshooting, and post-sale relationship building. NRF data shows retailers combining AI product-lookup tools with in-person consultative selling see stronger conversion rates than either channel alone [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The retail sales jobs with the longest shelf life are the ones that require real product expertise and in-person interaction: specialty retail (electronics, outdoor gear, automotive), showroom sales, and high-touch customer service roles. Generic cashier-plus-greeter positions are the most exposed to self-checkout and AI chat. Salesforce's research found that sales roles emphasizing relationship management and complex product knowledge were the least likely to see AI-driven headcount reductions [1]. If entering retail, target roles where explaining, demonstrating, or customizing the product is the main job, not just ringing it up.

quotes.quote: Sellers who use AI to handle research and data entry are spending 12% more time actually selling, and reporting higher quota attainment as a result.
The most effective sales teams are using AI for customer intelligence and then doubling their investment in face-to-face relationship building, not choosing one over the other.
Sales roles that combine product expertise with consultative interaction are seeing the strongest demand growth, even as transactional roles flatten.
Salespeople who develop skills in AI-assisted customer research alongside traditional product knowledge report faster ramp-up times and stronger early performance.

title: Insurance Sales Agents
keyDrivers: People buy insurance from someone they trust, especially for complex policies involving life, business, or major assets. The human relationship and ability to understand a client's unique situation provide real protection against automation. However, simpler insurance products are increasingly sold online without agents, and AI tools can now handle routine quoting and policy comparisons, which puts pressure on the lower end of this profession.

taskData.full: Sell various types of insurance policies to businesses and individuals on behalf of insurance companies, including automobile, fire, life, property, medical and dental insurance, or specialized policies, such as marine, farm/crop, and medical malpractice.
Customize insurance programs to suit individual customers, often covering a variety of risks.
Explain features, advantages, and disadvantages of various policies to promote sale of insurance plans.
Seek out new clients and develop clientele by networking to find new customers and generate lists of prospective clients.
Perform administrative tasks, such as maintaining records and handling policy renewals.
Call on policyholders to deliver and explain policy, to analyze insurance program and suggest additions or changes, or to change beneficiaries.
Select company that offers type of coverage requested by client to underwrite policy.
Calculate premiums and establish payment method.
Interview prospective clients to obtain data about their financial resources and needs, the physical condition of the person or property to be insured, and to discuss any existing coverage.
Plan and oversee incorporation of insurance program into bookkeeping system of company.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Direct-to-consumer insurance platforms like Lemonade, Policygenius, and embedded insurance at point of sale are compressing the role of the traditional agent in personal lines. McKinsey estimates that personal-lines distribution could see agent headcount decline as carriers shift to digital-first channels [1]. BLS projects just 6% growth for insurance sales agents through 2033, slower than the average for all occupations [2]. The biggest pressure point is simple, standardized products like auto and renters insurance, where comparison tools and AI-driven quoting engines let customers skip the agent entirely.
risks.statLabel: of personal-lines insurance purchases projected to be fully digital by 2025 [4]

opportunities.body: Complex commercial lines, life insurance, and high-net-worth personal coverage still depend heavily on relationship-driven advising that AI tools struggle to replicate. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 83% of sales representatives using AI tools reported revenue growth in the prior year, suggesting agents who adopt AI for prospecting and quoting can sell more, not less [3]. LIMRA research shows that the "advice gap" in life insurance remains wide: roughly 102 million U.S. adults say they need more life insurance coverage than they have, a demand pool that favors human advisors who can navigate underwriting complexity and emotional buying decisions [4].
opportunities.statLabel: U.S. adults say they need more life insurance [5]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-powered quoting and lead-scoring tools now. Platforms like Salesforce Einstein and carrier-specific CRM integrations can automate routine quote comparisons and flag high-intent prospects, freeing up time for consultative selling [3]. Over the next six months, build deeper expertise in complex product lines (commercial, life, umbrella) where the advisory relationship matters most. These are the areas where AI handles the least: understanding a client's full risk picture, navigating multi-carrier underwriting, and guiding emotionally loaded decisions like life insurance.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The strongest entry path is through commercial lines or life insurance, not personal auto or renters, where digital platforms are already dominant. Earning a state license is the baseline, but stacking a Chartered Property Casualty Underwriter (CPCU) or Certified Insurance Counselor (CIC) designation signals specialization that sets candidates apart. LIMRA's research on the advice gap shows that demand for human advisors is concentrated among clients with complex needs, so building a book in that space from the start is a better long-term bet than volume-based personal lines [4].

quotes.quote: Sales reps who use AI tools for lead prioritization and administrative tasks are 1.3× more likely to have seen revenue increase, the advantage comes from reinvesting that time in complex client conversations.

As insurers accelerate digital-direct channels for standard products, agents who reposition toward advisory roles in commercial and specialty lines capture higher commissions and stickier client relationships.
There are 102 million American adults who say they don't have enough life insurance, the need for knowledgeable advisors to close that gap isn't shrinking, it's growing.

The shift to digital quoting in personal lines means new agents should build their initial book around commercial accounts and complex personal coverage, where the advisory relationship is the product.

title: Sales Representatives, Wholesale and Manufacturing, Except Technical and Scientific Products
keyDrivers: Wholesale and manufacturing sales reps rely on relationship-building and product knowledge to serve their accounts, which offers some protection. However, many of their routine tasks, order processing, inventory checks, pricing, are being automated by e-commerce platforms and AI tools. The reps who survive will be those handling complex, high-value accounts where trust and negotiation still matter, but the overall number of positions is likely to shrink.

taskData.full: Answer customers' questions about products, prices, availability, product uses, and credit terms.
Recommend products to customers, based on customers' needs and interests.
Check stock levels and reorder merchandise as necessary.

Plan, assemble, and stock product displays in retail stores, or make recommendations to retailers regarding product displays, promotional programs, and advertising.

Prepare sales contracts and order forms.

Negotiate details of contracts and payments.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The two most important tasks in this role, answering customer questions and recommending products, already show automation rates of 37% and 33% respectively. That's significant because those tasks are the core of inside and routine sales work. AI-powered product configurators, chatbots, and CRM-driven recommendation engines are handling a growing share of what used to require a rep on the phone or in an email thread. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 81% of sales teams are either using or experimenting with generative AI, with lead qualification and customer communications among the top use cases [1]. The broader pattern: routine order-taking and informational sales calls are increasingly self-service, which compresses demand for reps whose value is primarily transactional.
risks.statLabel: of sales teams using or piloting generative AI [3]

opportunities.body: The augmentation signal on answering customer questions is 42%, the highest of any task with sufficient data in this role. That means AI is frequently used as a co-pilot rather than a replacement, helping reps pull up specs, pricing history, and competitive comparisons in real time. HubSpot's 2024 State of Sales report found that sales professionals using AI tools save roughly two hours per day on manual tasks like data entry and note-taking, freeing time for relationship-building and complex deal negotiation [2]. The tasks with no AI signal, negotiating contracts, managing retail merchandising relationships, and in-person client work, are structurally harder to automate because they depend on reading a room, building trust over time, and handling ambiguity. Reps who spend most of their time on consultative and relationship-driven selling are in a fundamentally different position than those doing transactional order fulfillment.
opportunities.statLabel: increase in meetings booked per rep using AI-assisted outreach tools [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools for pre-call research and proposal drafting now. CRM platforms like Salesforce and HubSpot already embed generative AI for call summaries, email drafts, and deal scoring, and reps who use them report saving roughly two hours a day on administrative work [2]. Over the next six months, focus on deepening consultative selling skills: complex multi-stakeholder negotiations, custom solution design, and in-person relationship management. These are the tasks where AI has no recorded activity and where the structural barriers to automation, reading body language, navigating politics, building trust, are highest.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The version of this role worth entering is consultative B2B sales, not transactional order-taking. Look for positions where the sales cycle is long, the deal size is large, and the product requires customization or technical explanation. When building a resume, demonstrate competency with CRM and AI-assisted sales tools like Salesforce Einstein or HubSpot's AI features. McKinsey's 2024 B2B sales research found that buyers increasingly favor reps who demonstrate deep understanding of their specific business challenges over those who lead with product features [3]. That's the skill that matters, and it's hard to fake on a resume without real examples of complex deals closed.

quotes.quote: Generative AI is helping sellers spend more time actually selling, top performers use AI for research, outreach, and CRM updates so they can focus on conversations that close deals.
Sales professionals who use AI to automate manual tasks like data entry and note-taking save an average of two hours and 15 minutes per day.
65% of B2B buyers say a rep who understands their specific business needs and pain points is more important than pricing in their purchase decision.
The highest-performing sales teams are 1.9 times more likely to use AI, but they pair it with deeper product expertise and account strategy, not just faster outreach.

title: Real Estate Sales Agents
keyDrivers: Sales agents show properties, build client relationships, and handle licensed transaction work, but much of their day-to-day involves tasks AI is rapidly improving at, lead generation, market comparisons, scheduling, and initial client matching. While licensing requirements and the importance of in-person showings provide some protection, agents face more displacement risk than brokers because they typically handle more routine transactions with less complex judgment. The large number of agents relative to market need makes this role vulnerable to consolidation as AI tools make each agent more productive.

taskData.full: Generate lists of properties that are compatible with buyers' needs and financial resources.
Promote sales of properties through advertisements, open houses, and participation in multiple listing services.
Prepare documents such as representation contracts, purchase agreements, closing statements, deeds, and leases.
Act as an intermediary in negotiations between buyers and sellers, generally representing one or the other.
Answer clients' questions regarding construction work, financing, maintenance, repairs, and appraisals.
Confer with escrow companies, lenders, home inspectors, and pest control operators to ensure that terms and conditions of purchase agreements are met before closing dates.
Compare a property with similar properties that have recently sold to determine its competitive market price.
Present purchase offers to sellers for consideration.
Coordinate appointments to show homes to prospective buyers.
Interview clients to determine what kinds of properties they are seeking.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The tasks AI hits hardest are the ones agents spend the most time on. Property matching (generating lists for buyers) shows 49% automation, marketing and listing promotion shows 41%, and answering client questions about financing and repairs shows 42%. These are core daily activities, not back-office busywork. NAR's 2024 member profile found that 35% of Realtors already use AI tools in their business, up from near zero two years prior [1]. Zillow and similar platforms are increasingly offering AI-powered home valuations and instant property recommendations directly to consumers, compressing the information advantage that agents have traditionally held [2].
risks.statLabel: active Realtors nationally, a saturated field where AI compresses agent differentiation [1]

opportunities.body: Negotiation, the task where agents act as intermediaries between buyers and sellers, shows a 29% automation rate That's the lowest automation rate among the core tasks with enough data, and the task itself depends on reading people, managing emotions, and navigating one-off deal structures that AI handles poorly. NAR data shows 88% of buyers purchased their home through an agent in 2024, and the top reason cited was help negotiating terms [1]. The augmentation signal on client Q&A (22%) suggests AI is useful as a research assistant for agents fielding questions about financing and appraisals, but clients still want a human explaining what it means for their specific situation. Transaction complexity also works in agents' favor: coordinating escrow, inspections, lenders, and pest control across a closing timeline involves real-time judgment calls that resist automation.
opportunities.statLabel: of buyers used an agent to purchase a home [1]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools for the property-matching and CMA (comparative market analysis) work that already shows high automation rates. Agents who let AI handle the initial search filtering and pricing comps can spend that time on the parts AI handles worst: face-to-face negotiation, reading client anxiety during walkthroughs, and managing the multi-party coordination of a closing. NAR found that agents using AI tools reported spending more time on client relationships and less on administrative tasks [1]. Over six months, building deeper expertise in complex transactions like multi-offer situations, 1031 exchanges, or investment property analysis creates a niche that automated platforms can't replicate.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The barrier to entry in real estate is low (a state license, typically a few weeks of coursework), but that's also the problem. Low barriers mean lots of competition, and AI is making the commodity part of the job (finding listings, pulling comps) nearly free. New agents who build a reputation around a specific transaction type or neighborhood tend to survive the first two years at much higher rates. Zillow's research shows that hyperlocal market knowledge remains one of the top reasons sellers choose a specific agent [2]. Focus early on learning the negotiation and closing coordination side, not the listing side that platforms are absorbing.

quotes.quote: Members who have integrated AI into their workflow report that they can better focus on the parts of real estate that clients most value — negotiation strategy, neighborhood expertise, and emotional support through complex transactions — rather than spending hours on property searches and CMA preparation.

Consumers increasingly start their search online, but they still turn to agents for the parts that require local expertise, negotiation skill, and trust. The agent value proposition is shifting from information access — which AI now provides — to judgment, relationships, and accountability through a high-stakes transaction.

The barrier to entry in real estate is low — a state license typically requires a few weeks of coursework — but that is also the problem. AI tools are compressing the information advantage that used to differentiate agents, so new entrants who build deep expertise in a specific geographic area or property type will have a clearer value proposition from day one.

The real estate landscape is shifting toward a model where technology handles discovery and initial filtering while agents handle the judgment-intensive work — evaluating properties in person, reading seller motivation, and navigating inspection and financing contingencies. New entrants who position around that judgment layer, rather than competing on access to listings, will be more durable.

title: Real Estate Brokers
keyDrivers: Real estate brokers are licensed professionals who bear legal responsibility for transactions, disclosures, and fiduciary duties to their clients, requirements that won't go away regardless of AI capabilities. The role is fundamentally built on trust, as people making the biggest financial decision of their lives want a human advocate who understands their needs and can negotiate on their behalf. While AI will streamline market analysis and paperwork, the broker's value in navigating complex deals and managing relationships between buyers, sellers, and other agents remains strong.

taskData.full: Supervise agents who handle real estate transactions.
Manage or operate real estate offices, handling associated business details.
Sell, for a fee, real estate owned by others.
Generate lists of properties for sale, their locations, descriptions, and available financing options, using computers.
Act as an intermediary in negotiations between buyers and sellers over property prices and settlement details and during the closing of sales.
Compare a property with similar properties that have recently sold to determine its competitive market price.
Monitor fulfillment of purchase contract terms to ensure that they are handled in a timely manner.
Obtain agreements from property owners to place properties for sale with real estate firms.
Rent properties or manage rental properties.
Maintain knowledge of real estate law, local economies, fair housing laws, types of available mortgages, financing options, and government programs.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Online platforms like Zillow, Redfin, and Opendoor have steadily absorbed functions that brokers once controlled exclusively, from property search to automated valuations to iBuying. NAR data shows total Realtor membership declined from a peak of roughly 1.56 million in 2022 to about 1.5 million by late 2024, reflecting compressed commissions and tighter transaction volumes after the 2023 commission lawsuit settlement [1]. BLS projects real estate broker employment to grow just 2% over the decade, roughly a third of the average for all occupations [2]. The combination of platform displacement and a legal shift toward negotiable commissions is squeezing the middle of the market hardest.

opportunities.body: NAR's 2024 buyer survey found that 88% of recent buyers purchased through an agent or broker, a figure that has barely moved in a decade despite the rise of online search tools [1]. Complex transactions, multi-property portfolios, and commercial deals still demand someone who can negotiate, interpret local regulations, and manage counterparty relationships. Brokers who use AI-powered CMA (comparative market analysis) tools and predictive analytics for lead scoring report closing deals faster. AI-generated listing descriptions and virtual staging tools are becoming standard practice, but interpreting local market conditions and advising on pricing strategy still requires experienced human judgment [3]. The brokerage model is shifting from gatekeeping information to providing judgment, local expertise, and transaction management that platforms cannot replicate.

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI-powered CMA and lead-scoring tools now. Platforms like HouseCanary can generate comps in seconds, but interpreting those comps for a specific deal still requires local knowledge and negotiation context [3]. Over the next six months, build depth in the parts of brokerage AI handles worst: complex commercial transactions, 1031 exchanges, multi-party negotiations, and local zoning interpretation. These tasks involve too many variables and too much interpersonal judgment for current AI to handle reliably.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The post-settlement commission landscape means new brokers need to demonstrate clear value from day one. Focus on a geographic niche or property type (luxury, multifamily, land) where deep local knowledge is a genuine differentiator rather than competing on volume in a commoditized residential market [1]. Getting a broker's license is table stakes. What separates successful entrants now is fluency with transaction technology, CRM systems, and data-driven pricing, combined with a network built through actual deal experience, not just coursework.

quotes.quote: Technology is raising the bar for what consumers expect. Brokers who adopt AI tools for CMA generation, lead scoring, and listing optimization handle more transactions with the same headcount — but the differentiation is moving to the judgment layer: deal structuring, agent coaching, and managing complex multi-party negotiations.

AI-enhanced listing descriptions generate meaningfully more saves on average, suggesting that the visual and content presentation layer of brokerage is already being commoditized. The durable broker advantage is in market interpretation and the trust-intensive work of guiding clients through high-stakes decisions under uncertainty.

The post-settlement commission landscape means new brokers need to demonstrate clear value from the first client conversation. Geographic specialization — knowing every sale, zoning decision, and school boundary change in a defined area — is a more defensible position than generalist coverage, especially as AI tools give buyers access to the same listings data brokers once controlled.

Real estate still generates roughly 37,000 openings a year — mostly from turnover, not growth. The brokers building durable businesses are the ones developing specialization: specific neighborhoods, property types, or buyer demographics. Generalist production-volume models are much more vulnerable to platform competition.

title: Sales Engineers
keyDrivers: Sales engineers combine deep technical expertise with the ability to build trust and navigate complex client relationships, which makes them hard to replace with AI. They must understand nuanced customer problems and craft custom solutions, often serving as the bridge between a company's engineering team and its clients. AI tools will actually make them more productive by handling research and proposals, while demand for their consultative expertise grows as products become more technically complex.

taskData.full: Sell products requiring extensive technical expertise and support for installation and use, such as material handling equipment, numerical-control machinery, or computer systems.
Develop, present, or respond to proposals for specific customer requirements, including request for proposal responses and industry-specific solutions.
Collaborate with sales teams to understand customer requirements, to promote the sale of company products, and to provide sales support.
Keep informed on industry news and trends, products, services, competitors, relevant information about legacy, existing, and emerging technologies, and the latest product-line developments.
Document account activities, generate reports, and keep records of business transactions with customers and suppliers.
Confer with customers and engineers to assess equipment needs and to determine system requirements.
Identify resale opportunities and support them to achieve sales plans.
Create sales or service contracts for products or services.
Recommend improved materials or machinery to customers, documenting how such changes will lower costs or increase production.
Plan and modify product configurations to meet customer needs.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The tasks where AI shows the strongest automation signal are proposal development (29% automation rate) and sales-team collaboration (44% automation rate), both core activities in the role. AI tools can now draft initial proposal responses, generate competitive comparisons, and pull together technical specs without a human starting from scratch. Salesforce's 2024 State of Sales report found that 81% of sales teams are either using or experimenting with generative AI, with the most common use cases being content generation for proposals and email outreach [1]. The broader sales engineering function still requires deep product knowledge and live customer interaction, but the prep work that used to fill much of a sales engineer's week is shrinking.
risks.statLabel: of sales teams using or piloting generative AI [1]

opportunities.body: The strongest augmentation signal sits in needs assessment conversations with customers and engineers, where 42% of AI interactions show augmentation patterns and success rates reach 65% [2]. This means AI is helping sales engineers prepare better for technical discovery calls, not replacing the calls themselves. Harvard Business Review found that sales teams using AI for customer research and demo preparation report stronger win rates, particularly in technical sales where personalized presentations are the core differentiator [3]. The ability to translate complex engineering constraints into business value during live conversations remains structurally hard to automate because it requires reading a room, adapting on the fly, and holding accountability for the recommendation.
opportunities.statLabel: success rate on AI-assisted needs assessment conversations [1]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools to draft proposal responses and competitive analyses now. The 29% automation rate on proposal work means these tools are already good enough to produce a solid first draft, freeing up time for the parts AI handles worst. Over the next six months, invest that reclaimed time in deepening live technical consultation skills, especially complex multi-stakeholder discovery sessions where the sales engineer has to synthesize engineering constraints with business goals in real time [2]. That's where the augmentation signal is strongest and where human judgment stays essential.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The entry path into sales engineering increasingly rewards candidates who can demonstrate both technical depth and consultative selling ability. Build a portfolio that shows real technical problem-solving: contribute to open-source projects, write up case studies of systems you've configured or troubleshot, and practice explaining complex technical tradeoffs to non-technical audiences. Research shows that personalized, consultative interactions remain the biggest differentiator for top-performing sales engineers [3]. Pair that with hands-on fluency in AI-assisted proposal tools so you walk in already able to work the way the role is heading.

quotes.quote: Sales engineers who are winning deals in an AI-heavy product era are the ones who can translate technical complexity for buyers — not just explain what the product does, but help the customer understand what it means for their specific workflow. That translating role is not something a demo or chatbot replaces.
quotes.attribution: Harvard Business Review, "How AI Is Changing the Game for Sales Teams"

quotes.quote: The most in-demand sales engineers right now are the ones who can speak to AI integration — how the product connects to existing data pipelines, what the implementation looks like, what the realistic ROI is. That technical credibility in the room is the core value prop of the role.

Sales engineering sits at the intersection of technical depth and commercial fluency — and the path in is usually through a technical role first. Engineers who develop customer-facing communication skills on the job, or through solutions architect experience, are the strongest candidates for SE roles.
quotes.attribution: Harvard Business Review, "How AI Is Changing the Game for Sales Teams"

quotes.quote: Technical certification in the product domain — cloud platforms, data infrastructure, security — is the clearest signal for sales engineer hiring. Buyers expect SEs to have depth, not just familiarity, and the credentialing structures in cloud and security give candidates a concrete way to demonstrate it.

howToAdapt_quotes.quote: Seventy-eight percent of salespeople who use AI say it helps them spend more time on actual selling... the time savings come primarily from automating research, prep work, and content drafting.

Sales engineers who combine product expertise with consultative discovery skills consistently outperform peers who rely on technical demos alone... AI is accelerating this shift by handling the routine technical documentation.
The most in-demand sales skills are now AI tool fluency and the ability to provide personalized, insight-driven customer experiences rather than scripted product walkthroughs.
High performers are 1.9x more likely to use AI for prospect research and solution configuration, suggesting that AI literacy is becoming a baseline expectation rather than a differentiator.

title: Sales Managers
keyDrivers: Sales managers lead teams, set strategy, and navigate complex organizational politics, work that depends heavily on reading people, building coalitions, and making high-stakes judgment calls about markets and talent. Their deep knowledge of their company's culture, clients, and competitive landscape is hard for AI to replicate. AI will enhance their effectiveness by automating forecasting and reporting, letting them focus more on coaching and strategic decisions.

taskData.full: Oversee regional and local sales managers and their staffs.
Resolve customer complaints regarding sales and service.
Confer with potential customers regarding equipment needs, and advise customers on types of equipment to purchase.
Direct and coordinate activities involving sales of manufactured products, services, commodities, real estate, or other subjects of sale.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The one task with meaningful AI data, resolving customer complaints, shows a 28% automation rate. That's notable because AI-powered chatbots and CRM tools are already handling front-line complaint triage and routing, reducing the volume of issues that require a manager's direct involvement [1]. Salesforce reports that 81% of sales teams are either experimenting with or have implemented AI, with lead qualification and pipeline forecasting among the most common use cases [2]. As those tools mature, the analytical and reporting side of sales management, reviewing records, projecting sales, tracking profitability, faces real compression. The broader concern isn't job elimination but role thinning: fewer managers needed per sales team when AI handles forecasting, territory optimization, and performance dashboards automatically.
risks.statLabel: of sales teams experimenting with or using AI [1]

opportunities.body: The core of this role, directing people, building customer relationships, negotiating complex deals, and coaching teams, has almost no AI footprint. Harvard Business Review found that while AI tools improve customer intelligence and pipeline analytics, closing deals and managing team relationships remain fundamentally human tasks that AI handles poorly [3]. Sales managers who use AI for pipeline analytics and lead scoring report spending more time on strategic selling and team development, not less time working [2]. The management and coordination tasks that dominate this role's core work, staffing, training, cross-department planning, require the kind of contextual judgment and accountability that AI handles poorly.
opportunities.statLabel: of salespeople say AI improves customer understanding [1]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start by getting fluent in the AI tools the sales team is already using or about to adopt. CRM-embedded AI features like Salesforce Einstein or HubSpot's predictive lead scoring are the fastest way to see what the data actually shows versus what reps report anecdotally [2]. Over six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst: coaching underperformers through slumps, negotiating custom enterprise deals, and reading the political dynamics inside a client's buying committee. These tasks barely register in AI usage data because they require real-time social judgment that no model replicates well.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The path into sales management almost always runs through a few years as an individual contributor first. When building that track record, focus on complex or consultative sales (enterprise software, medical devices, industrial equipment) rather than transactional sales, since transactional pipelines are the first to get AI-automated [2]. Demonstrating that you can run a forecast, diagnose why a deal stalled, and coach a junior rep through it is more valuable than any single certification. Familiarity with CRM analytics and AI-driven pipeline tools is table stakes for new managers now, so get hands-on with them as a rep, not after promotion [3].

quotes.quote: Sales leaders are now expected to interpret AI-generated insights, not just review dashboards, and translate them into coaching moments for their teams.
The top-performing salespeople don't just use AI to save time, they use the time saved to deepen relationships and handle more complex deals.
Reps who actively use AI tools for prospecting and pipeline management are promoted to management roles faster, because they already think in terms of data-driven strategy rather than gut instinct.
As AI automates routine pipeline tasks, the premium shifts to managers who can handle ambiguity, reading a room, structuring a creative deal, motivating a demoralized team.

title: Database Architects
keyDrivers: Database architects do more strategic, cross-functional design work than administrators, which requires understanding business needs and making judgment calls about system trade-offs. However, the work is still fully digital with no licensure, and AI tools are getting better at suggesting optimal architectures. The growing explosion of data and AI systems does create more demand for people who can design the underlying infrastructure, which provides a meaningful upside.

taskData.full: Collaborate with system architects, software architects, design analysts, and others to understand business or industry requirements.
Develop and document database architectures.
Design databases to support business applications, ensuring system scalability, security, performance, and reliability.
Work as part of a project team to coordinate database development and determine project scope and limitations.
Develop database architectural strategies at the modeling, design and implementation stages to address business or industry requirements.

Design database applications, such as interfaces, data transfer mechanisms, global temporary tables, data partitions, and function-based indexes to enable efficient access of the generic database structure.
Develop data models for applications, metadata tables, views or related database structures.
Develop data model describing data elements and their use, following procedures and using pen, template or computer software.
Develop methods for integrating different products so they work properly together, such as customizing commercial databases to fit specific needs.
Document and communicate database schemas, using accepted notations.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The tasks with the highest automation signals are the hands-on technical ones: designing interfaces and data transfer mechanisms (23% automation, ~1,000 conversations) and developing data models (21% automation). These are the bread-and-butter build tasks that junior database architects spend most of their time on. AI code-generation tools can now scaffold schemas, write DDL, and generate boilerplate migration scripts with minimal human input. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey found that 76% of developers are using or planning to use AI tools in their workflow [1], and database work is squarely in the sweet spot for these tools. BLS projects only 9% growth for this occupation over the next decade [2], which is moderate but not fast enough to absorb displaced entry-level demand if AI keeps compressing the routine build work.
risks.statLabel: of organizations now use managed database services, reducing hands-on DBA work [4]

opportunities.body: The strongest signal here is augmentation, not automation. The most important task, collaborating with system architects and stakeholders to understand business requirements, shows 56% augmentation against only 14% automation across 556 conversations [1]. Developing architectural strategies shows a similar pattern: 51% augmentation, 13% automation [1]. This means AI is useful as a drafting partner for these tasks but humans are still directing the outcome. Database architects who can translate ambiguous business needs into technical architecture are doing work that AI assists but doesn't replace. The WEF Future of Jobs Report 2025 lists analytical thinking and AI/big-data skills among the fastest-growing skill demands for technology roles [3], and this role sits at the intersection of both.
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings, concentrated in roles requiring business-to-technical translation [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI assistants for schema generation and migration scripting now. The 23% automation rate on design tasks [1] means these tools already work; learning their failure modes firsthand is more valuable than reading about them. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst for this role: translating vague business requirements into architectural decisions, and designing for cross-system integration where the constraints are organizational, not technical. The 51% augmentation rate on architecture strategy tasks [1] shows AI can help draft options, but choosing between them still requires understanding the business context that prompted the project.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: A portfolio that shows real database architecture decisions, not just schema diagrams, will stand out. Build a project where a database serves multiple applications with conflicting performance requirements, and document why each tradeoff was made. Cloud certifications in AWS, Azure, or GCP database services are table stakes for entry, but the WEF report flags analytical thinking as the top growing skill demand across tech roles [3], so demonstrating that through case-study writeups matters more than collecting certificates. Pair database fundamentals with enough application-layer knowledge to have informed conversations with software architects.

quotes.quote: 76% of all developers are either currently using or are planning to use AI coding tools in their development process.
Analytical thinking tops the list of growing core skills, followed by resilience, flexibility and agility, and AI and big data skills.
Technology-related roles such as Big Data Specialists, AI and Machine Learning Specialists, and Data Engineers feature prominently among the fastest-growing roles.
Developers using AI coding tools report spending more time on system design and architecture decisions as routine implementation becomes faster.

title: Computer Systems Engineers/Architects
keyDrivers: Systems architects make high-stakes design decisions about complex infrastructure where mistakes can be costly and situations are often genuinely novel. AI will handle more routine design patterns, but someone still needs to make the big-picture architectural calls and integrate competing constraints. These professionals are also well-positioned to direct and validate AI-generated designs, keeping them relevant as AI tools expand what's possible.

taskData.full: Communicate with staff or clients to understand specific system requirements.
Monitor system operation to detect potential problems.
Direct the analysis, development, and operation of complete computer systems.
Investigate system component suitability for specified purposes, and make recommendations regarding component use.
Direct the installation of operating systems, network or application software, or computer or network hardware.
Perform ongoing hardware and software maintenance operations, including installing or upgrading hardware or software.
Provide customers or installation teams guidelines for implementing secure systems.
Provide technical guidance or support for the development or troubleshooting of systems.
Identify system data, hardware, or software components required to meet user needs.
Verify stability, interoperability, portability, security, or scalability of system architecture.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The maintenance and installation side of this role shows real automation pressure. Routine hardware and software maintenance tasks have a 44% automation rate in AI conversations, meaning AI tools are already handling a meaningful share of patching, upgrades, and configuration work without human involvement. Infrastructure-as-code platforms and AI-assisted provisioning tools are accelerating this trend. GitHub's 2024 Octoverse report found that AI-generated code now accounts for a growing share of commits in infrastructure repositories, with Copilot adoption among DevOps and infrastructure engineers rising sharply [1]. The broader systems engineering labor market is projected to grow just 3% over the next decade according to BLS data [2], which is slower than average and suggests muted new-job creation even as existing roles shift.
risks.statLabel: of organizations adopted infrastructure-as-code, automating routine system maintenance [4]

opportunities.body: Technical guidance and troubleshooting is the strongest augmentation signal in this role. That task shows a 63% augmentation rate across over 1,500 AI conversations, meaning engineers are using AI as a research and diagnostic partner rather than handing off the work entirely [1]. This pattern fits: designing and debugging complex distributed systems requires contextual judgment that AI assists but can't own. The 2024 Stack Overflow Developer Survey found that 76% of developers are using or plan to use AI tools in their workflow, but only 43% trust AI output for architectural decisions [3]. Systems architecture work, where the role involves evaluating tradeoffs across security, scalability, and cost, scores high on specialized expertise (A9: 3) and AI-direction capability (A10: 5), both durable advantages.
opportunities.statLabel: annual job openings across computer systems roles, driven by cloud migration complexity [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI coding assistants for infrastructure-as-code reviews and troubleshooting workflows now. The data shows AI is already a strong partner for technical guidance tasks, with a 63% augmentation rate [1]. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst for this role: cross-system architecture tradeoff analysis, security posture evaluation, and stakeholder requirement translation. These tasks involve ambiguity and organizational context that AI tools consistently struggle with.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Entry-level infrastructure work, the maintenance and provisioning layer, is the part most exposed to automation. A stronger entry point is building a portfolio around system design: document architecture decisions for real or simulated multi-service deployments, including why alternatives were rejected. The Stack Overflow survey shows employers still don't trust AI for architectural decisions [3], so demonstrated design judgment stands out. Cloud provider certifications (AWS Solutions Architect, Azure Solutions Architect Expert) remain useful signals, but pair them with hands-on projects that show tradeoff reasoning, not just configuration knowledge.

quotes.quote: Developers are integrating AI into their workflows at a rapid pace , 97% of respondents have used AI coding tools at some point. The shift is toward using these tools for code generation, debugging, and documentation rather than replacing design work.

AI-powered code generation is becoming embedded in the developer workflow, with Copilot now active in infrastructure and DevOps repositories at growing rates, but adoption is highest in routine commits, not architectural changes.

Only 43% of developers trust AI tools for complex tasks like architectural decisions, compared to much higher trust for code completion and documentation, suggesting that design skills remain a differentiator.
The fastest-growing repositories in 2024 involved infrastructure-as-code and platform engineering, areas where AI accelerates routine work but human review of system-level tradeoffs remains the norm.

title: Bookkeeping, Accounting, and Auditing Clerks
keyDrivers: Bookkeeping and accounting clerk work is highly structured and rule-based, recording transactions, reconciling accounts, and generating reports, making it one of the most automatable roles as AI tools handle these tasks faster and more accurately. There is no requirement for physical presence, licensure, or high-stakes novel judgment. AI doesn't create new demand for this role; instead, it directly replaces the core tasks, leaving very little protection.

taskData.full: Operate computers programmed with accounting software to record, store, and analyze information.
Operate 10-key calculators, typewriters, and copy machines to perform calculations and produce documents.
Check figures, postings, and documents for correct entry, mathematical accuracy, and proper codes.
Perform general office duties, such as filing, answering telephones, and handling routine correspondence.
Comply with federal, state, and company policies, procedures, and regulations.
Code documents according to company procedures.
Receive, record, and bank cash, checks, and vouchers.
Prepare bank deposits by compiling data from cashiers, verifying and balancing receipts, and sending cash, checks, or other forms of payment to banks.
Calculate and prepare checks for utilities, taxes, and other payments.
Perform financial calculations, such as amounts due, interest charges, balances, discounts, equity, and principal.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: BLS projects bookkeeping clerk employment to decline 6% over the next decade, a loss of roughly 100,000 positions, as accounting software increasingly handles data entry, reconciliation, and basic reporting [1]. The WEF Future of Jobs Report names accounting and bookkeeping clerks among the fastest-declining roles globally, driven by AI and automation performing the structured, repetitive work that defines the occupation [2]. The financial calculations task already shows 57.7% automation in AI usage data, and that task represents the analytical core of the role.
risks.statLabel: of accounting firms adopted automation technologies in the past year [3]

opportunities.body: The Intuit QuickBooks 2025 survey found that 81% of accountants report AI boosts their productivity, with firms using automation reporting near-unanimous improvements in accuracy (98%) and efficiency (97%) [3]. This creates demand for bookkeepers who can operate AI-powered tools and interpret results rather than just enter data. The AICPA reports that AI is accelerating the shift from compliance-focused bookkeeping toward strategic advisory services, a transition that opens new value for clerks willing to move up the skill ladder [4].
opportunities.statLabel: of accountants report AI boosts their productivity [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Learn the AI features in whatever accounting platform you use daily. QuickBooks, Xero, and Sage all now offer automated categorization, anomaly flagging, and reconciliation. Start there rather than chasing a separate AI certification [3]. Over the next six months, build advisory skills: interpreting reports for clients, spotting cash flow trends, and communicating financial insights. The AICPA notes that the profession is shifting from transaction processing to strategic advising, and bookkeepers who can bridge both sides are in the strongest position [4].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Target employers and roles that emphasize cloud accounting and client interaction rather than pure data entry. Those positions are the most durable. BLS still projects 170,000 annual openings for bookkeeping clerks despite the overall decline, nearly all from replacement needs as experienced workers retire [1]. The WEF Future of Jobs Report highlights that while clerical roles are declining, the skills gap in AI-literate finance workers is widening, creating an opening for new entrants who arrive with both accounting fundamentals and platform proficiency [2].

title: Tax Preparers
keyDrivers: Most tax preparation work follows well-defined rules that AI can already handle faster and more accurately than humans. While some clients value a personal relationship with their preparer, the core task, applying tax code to financial data, is highly automatable. This role faces significant displacement as AI-powered tax software continues to improve.

taskData.full: Compute taxes owed or overpaid, using adding machines or personal computers, and complete entries on forms, following tax form instructions and tax tables.
Use all appropriate adjustments, deductions, and credits to keep clients' taxes to a minimum.
Prepare or assist in preparing simple to complex tax returns for individuals or small businesses.
Furnish taxpayers with sufficient information and advice to ensure correct tax form completion.
Interview clients to obtain additional information on taxable income and deductible expenses and allowances.
Review financial records, such as income statements and documentation of expenditures to determine forms needed to prepare tax returns.
Check data input or verify totals on forms prepared by others to detect errors in arithmetic, data entry, or procedures.
Explain federal and state tax laws to individuals and companies.
Answer questions and provide future tax planning to clients.
Calculate form preparation fees according to return complexity and processing time required.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The structural threat to tax preparers is not AI replacing professionals but AI-powered consumer software replacing the client. TurboTax, H&R Block, and similar platforms now handle most straightforward W-2 returns with embedded AI guidance, shrinking the addressable market for simple seasonal prep work [1]. BLS still projects faster-than-average employment growth (5–6%) overall, but that growth is concentrated in small-business, self-employment, and complex return work where software falls short [1]. Thomson Reuters found that 44% of tax firms using generative AI already deploy it daily, compressing the skill floor while raising the ceiling for what clients expect [2].
risks.statLabel: of tax firms using generative AI deploy it daily [2]

opportunities.body: The 72.8% augmentation rate on the explaining-tax-laws task is a durable signal: AI surfaces answers quickly, but clients need someone to interpret those answers and connect them to their specific situation [3]. Enrolled Agent (EA) and CPA credentials unlock IRS representation rights, the one category of tax work that software can't perform. The Intuit 2025 survey found 93% of accounting firms now use AI to enhance strategic advisory services, and firms making this shift report higher revenue per client [3]. The AICPA sees the profession accelerating toward advisory, where judgment and relationship skills define the value delivered [4].
opportunities.statLabel: of accounting firms use AI to enhance advisory services [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using whatever AI research tools are built into your tax software platform. Drake, Lacerte, and ProConnect all now include AI-assisted research features. Use them to handle the lookup work so your time goes to the judgment calls AI cannot make [3]. Over the next six months, pursue the Enrolled Agent credential if you do not have it. EA gives unlimited IRS representation rights and positions you for audits, collections, and appeals work that self-service software will never touch. The AICPA is clear that advisory services are where the profession is heading [4].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Do not enter tax prep as a seasonal W-2 filer. That segment is being compressed by consumer software. Instead, target small business, self-employed, and investment-income returns where the tax picture is complex enough that clients need ongoing professional help [1]. Get an AFSP (Annual Filing Season Program) designation first to establish IRS credentials, then pursue the EA exam. Thomson Reuters found that professionals who adopt a visible AI strategy are twice as likely to see revenue growth, entering with both technical credentials and AI fluency puts you ahead of incumbents who are still catching up [2].
