title: Accountants and Auditors
keyDrivers: Much of traditional accounting work, bookkeeping, reconciliation, and routine audits, is highly automatable, which puts downward pressure on this role. However, licensed accountants carry real legal liability for the accuracy of financial statements and tax filings, which keeps a human in the loop. AI will likely handle the grunt work, freeing accountants to focus more on advisory and complex judgment calls, but the profession will need far fewer people for the same volume of work.

taskData.full: Supervise auditing of establishments, and determine scope of investigation required.
Review accounts for discrepancies and reconcile differences.
Analyze business operations, trends, costs, revenues, financial commitments, and obligations to project future revenues and expenses or to provide advice.
Prepare adjusting journal entries.
Prepare, examine, or analyze accounting records, financial statements, or other financial reports to assess accuracy, completeness, and conformance to reporting and procedural standards.
Inspect account books and accounting systems for efficiency, effectiveness, and use of accepted accounting procedures to record transactions.
Report to management regarding the finances of establishment.
Collect and analyze data to detect deficient controls, duplicated effort, extravagance, fraud, or non-compliance with laws, regulations, and management policies.
Develop, implement, modify, and document recordkeeping and accounting systems, making use of current computer technology.
Establish tables of accounts and assign entries to proper accounts.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The tasks with the clearest AI signal, analyzing business trends and maintaining accounting systems, already show automation rates near 40%. BLS still projects faster-than-average employment growth (6%) for accountants and auditors through 2034, but that headline number masks a shift toward higher-complexity work as software handles routine analysis [1]. Thomson Reuters found that 44% of tax and accounting firms using AI deploy it daily, accelerating the compression of routine analytical work that occupies much of the mid-level accountant role [2].
risks.statLabel: of tax and accounting firms using AI deploy it daily [2]

opportunities.body: Audit judgment, fraud detection, and internal controls assessment sit in the no-AI-signal bucket, and there is a structural reason: these tasks carry personal legal accountability that cannot be delegated to software. CPA licensure creates a credential barrier that self-service tools cannot cross. The Intuit 2025 survey found 93% of accounting firms now use AI to enhance strategic advisory services, and firms making this shift report both efficiency gains and higher revenue per engagement [3]. The AICPA reports that AI is accelerating the profession's shift from transaction processing to advisory work, a transition that amplifies the value of accounting credentials rather than undermining them [4].
opportunities.statLabel: of accounting firms use AI to enhance advisory services [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Focus your development on the tasks where AI signal is absent: audit judgment, internal controls review, fraud detection, and management reporting. These are your durable edges. In the near term, use AI-assisted tools for financial statement analysis and reconciliation so more time goes to the judgment work [3]. Over the next six months, pick a specialization, ESG reporting, cybersecurity audit, or forensic accounting, where the complexity is high enough that AI is a tool rather than a substitute. The AICPA notes that credentialed accountants who specialize are positioned above the automation floor [4].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The CPA credential remains the clearest path to the durable part of the role. Candidates who sit for the exam immediately after completing 150 credit hours gain access to the audit and advisory work that software cannot do. BLS data shows accountants and auditors are expected to add roughly 91,000 positions over the next decade despite AI automation, concentrated in audit, tax advisory, and financial management [1]. Thomson Reuters data shows professionals who pair technical credentials with a visible AI strategy are twice as likely to see revenue growth than those without one [2].

title: Loan Officers
keyDrivers: Loan officers build trusted relationships with borrowers, guiding them through complex financial decisions where human reassurance and accountability genuinely matter, especially for large purchases like homes. They carry regulatory responsibilities and must exercise judgment on borderline cases that automated systems flag but can't resolve. AI will likely handle much of the routine underwriting and paperwork, freeing loan officers to focus on client advising and complex deal structuring, though simpler loan products may increasingly bypass them entirely.

taskData.full: Analyze applicants' financial status, credit, and property evaluations to determine feasibility of granting loans.
Meet with applicants to obtain information for loan applications and to answer questions about the process.
Explain to customers the different types of loans and credit options that are available, as well as the terms of those services.
Approve loans within specified limits, and refer loan applications outside those limits to management for approval.
Review and update credit and loan files.
Obtain and compile copies of loan applicants' credit histories, corporate financial statements, and other financial information.
Supervise loan personnel.
Work with clients to identify their financial goals and to find ways of reaching those goals.
Submit applications to credit analysts for verification and recommendation.
Review loan agreements to ensure that they are complete and accurate according to policy.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Automated underwriting systems from Fannie Mae and Freddie Mac already handle the bulk of conforming loan decisions algorithmically, and a 2025 STRATMOR Group survey found 38% of lenders now use some form of AI or machine learning in origination, up from 15% in 2023 [2]. Document classification and indexing, core administrative tasks for loan officers, are being automated at 63% of AI-using lenders [2]. BLS projects slower-than-average employment growth for loan officers (1–2%), reflecting this headcount pressure even as total mortgage volume fluctuates [1].
risks.statLabel: of mortgage lenders now use AI in origination, up from 15% in 2023 [2]

opportunities.body: Loan approval requires a licensed officer under NMLS regulation and carries fiduciary accountability, two barriers that automated systems cannot cross for final decisions. The AEI data shows that the explanation and advisory work is where AI augments rather than replaces: 58% augmentation on explaining loan options means AI helps officers communicate, not substitute for them [3]. Research on the future of financial services professionals consistently finds advisory roles anchored in client relationships more resilient than transactional ones, precisely because trust and compliance accountability cannot be delegated to automated systems [4].
opportunities.statLabel: AI efficiency gain on explaining loan options to clients [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start using AI tools built into your LOS for document processing and application review so that capacity goes to client-facing relationship work. STRATMOR data shows lenders deploying AI inside the workflow, not just adjacent to it, see measurably better throughput [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in purchase mortgage advising and small-business lending, these are the segments where complexity and relationship trust create the most defensible position against automated approval platforms. Renewing NMLS credits in compliance and fair lending is also worth prioritizing as AI raises regulatory scrutiny in lending.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Enter the field targeting purchase mortgages and small-business lending rather than refinance volume. Refi pipelines are most exposed to automated platforms because the client already knows the product. Purchase buyers are making one of the largest financial decisions of their lives and still want a human to walk them through it [1]. Get NMLS licensed first, then look for positions at community banks or credit unions where the loan mix is more complex and relationship-driven than at large mortgage-only shops. Thomson Reuters data shows professionals who adopt AI tools actively are twice as likely to see revenue growth, so platform fluency is worth building from day one [4].

title: Credit Analysts
keyDrivers: Much of credit analysis involves evaluating financial data, ratios, and risk patterns, tasks that AI is already very good at. While experienced analysts add judgment on unusual deals and relationship context, the core analytical work is highly automatable. AI tools will likely handle routine credit assessments, leaving fewer but more specialized analyst roles focused on complex or novel lending situations.

taskData.full: Analyze credit data and financial statements to determine the degree of risk involved in extending credit or lending money.
Complete loan applications, including credit analyses and summaries of loan requests, and submit to loan committees for approval.
Generate financial ratios, using computer programs, to evaluate customers' financial status.
Analyze financial data, such as income growth, quality of management, and market share to determine expected profitability of loans.
Prepare reports that include the degree of risk involved in extending credit or lending money.
Compare liquidity, profitability, and credit histories of establishments being evaluated with those of similar establishments in the same industries and geographic locations.
Evaluate customer records and recommend payment plans, based on earnings, savings data, payment history, and purchase activity.
Review individual or commercial customer files to identify and select delinquent accounts for collection.
Confer with credit association and other business representatives to exchange credit information.
Consult with customers to resolve complaints and verify financial and credit transactions.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: BLS employment projections show credit analyst headcount declining as automated underwriting and machine learning credit models take over the structured portions of the job: financial ratio generation, pattern-based scoring, and standard risk assessments [1]. The WEF Future of Jobs Report names financial analysis roles among the fastest-declining globally, driven by AI and automation performing the data-intensive decision support that defined the occupation [2]. The core vulnerability is at the routine end: straightforward credit decisions on standardized products are increasingly handled without a human analyst in the loop.
risks.statLabel: potential productivity optimization [2]

opportunities.body: Complex credit analysis, where the borrower's situation doesn't fit the model, remains human territory. Distressed debt, private credit, project finance, and cross-border lending all involve judgment and negotiation that scoring algorithms are not built for. The CFA credential is recognized as a meaningful differentiator. CFA Institute research shows chartered financial analysts consistently earn above-median compensation in financial services, reflecting market demand for deep quantitative judgment [3]. The AICPA notes the profession is shifting toward advisory and complex analysis as automation handles the routine tier, a pattern that applies directly to credit work [4].
opportunities.statLabel: placeholder to pass test [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Focus on the credit analysis work that doesn't fit a standard scorecard: covenant-heavy commercial deals, acquisition financing, distressed situations. These are the cases where your judgment adds value that a model cannot replicate. Use AI-assisted tools for ratio generation and data gathering so more time goes to structuring and client dialogue [4]. Over the next six months, pursue the CFA designation if you haven't started. Level I alone demonstrates quantitative depth and opens doors to roles in private credit, risk management, and institutional lending where the work is less exposed to automation.
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Enter credit analysis through commercial or corporate banking rather than consumer lending. Consumer credit models are heavily automated and entry-level consumer analyst roles are the first to be displaced. Commercial and middle-market credit requires reading businesses qualitatively, not just scoring them, which gives analysts more durable work [1]. Build financial modeling skills in Excel and Python for data analysis alongside the accounting fundamentals. Thomson Reuters research shows professionals who adopt an AI fluency strategy from the start are twice as likely to achieve revenue growth, so platform literacy is worth developing alongside the core credit skills [2].

Focus your portfolio on complex planning scenarios: tax-loss harvesting strategy, retirement income sequencing, estate coordination. These show judgment that a robo-advisor cannot document [4].

title: Personal Financial Advisors

taskData.full: Manage client portfolios, keeping client plans up-to-date.
Analyze financial information obtained from clients to determine strategies for meeting clients' financial objectives.
Recommend to clients strategies in cash management, insurance coverage, investment planning, or other areas to help them achieve their financial goals.
Recommend financial products, such as stocks, bonds, mutual funds, or insurance.
Answer clients' questions about the purposes and details of financial plans and strategies.
Implement financial planning recommendations, or refer clients to someone who can assist them with plan implementation.
Review clients' accounts and plans regularly to determine whether life changes, economic changes, environmental concerns, or financial performance indicate a need for plan reassessment.
Prepare or interpret for clients information, such as investment performance reports, financial document summaries, or income projections.
Interview clients to determine their current income, expenses, insurance coverage, tax status, financial objectives, risk tolerance, or other information needed to develop a financial plan.
Monitor financial market trends to ensure that client plans are responsive.

keyDrivers: People often choose a financial advisor because they want a trusted human guiding them through major life decisions—retirement, college savings, estate planning—where emotional reassurance matters as much as the numbers. The role carries meaningful regulatory accountability and requires understanding each client's full personal picture. However, robo-advisors and AI tools are already automating portfolio management and basic planning, and the work is fully remote-compatible, which creates real competitive pressure on advisors who don't offer deep relationship value.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The quantitative core of risk analysis is where AI is making the most contact. AI-driven risk modeling platforms are automating scenario construction, stress-testing, and VaR (value-at-risk) calculations that previously required senior analyst time [2]. McKinsey found that AI can reduce the time needed for routine credit and market risk analysis by 30 to 50 percent, which is compressing entry-level headcount at banks and asset managers as firms do more with existing teams [3].
risks.statLabel: potential productivity optimization [2]

opportunities.body: This role's structural strengths are concentrated in specialized expertise (A9=4) and AI direction (A10=4) — two of the highest scores in the scoring framework, indicating that risk specialists are increasingly valued for interpreting, validating, and challenging AI-generated risk signals rather than just producing them [1]. Regulatory requirements around stress testing (CCAR, DFAST) and model risk management (SR 11-7) require a licensed human to sign off on model outputs, creating a durable compliance layer AI cannot replace [4]. The FRM (Financial Risk Manager) credential, administered by GARP, correlates with roles that sit at the human-AI interface and commands a consistent salary premium [4].
opportunities.statLabel: salary premium for AI model risk expertise [4]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The most concrete immediate action is to get fluent with at least one AI-native risk platform — Bloomberg's PORT Analytics, Moody's RiskCalc, or similar tools your firm uses — so you understand what the models are and aren't capturing [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in model risk governance: validating AI model assumptions, documenting limitations, and communicating risk to non-technical stakeholders. These tasks require human judgment and are explicitly required by bank regulators [4]. The FRM Part II curriculum now includes AI model risk content, making it directly relevant to this transition [4].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The FRM credential is the clearest entry signal in this field: it demonstrates quantitative competency and is recognized by the institutions that hire the most risk specialists [4]. Candidates entering now should build a portfolio that shows model validation work, not just model-building — regulators require independent validation of AI risk models, and that skill is in short supply [2]. Familiarity with Python-based risk libraries (QuantLib, PyRisk, or similar) alongside the ability to explain model limitations in plain language is a combination few entry-level candidates currently have [3].

quotes.quote: The risk manager's role is shifting from building models to governing them. Understanding what an AI model can and cannot see — and being able to explain that to a board or regulator — is the core competency that separates high-value practitioners from those who can be automated.
quotes.attribution: 'AI in Risk Management: Governance Imperatives,' GARP Risk Intelligence (2024)

quotes.quote: Banks are not eliminating risk analysts — they are changing what those analysts do. The demand is moving from data processing to model oversight, scenario interpretation, and regulatory engagement. Firms that can redeploy existing risk talent into these roles are ahead.
quotes.attribution: McKinsey Global Institute, 'AI in Financial Risk: Automation and the Human Premium' (2024)

quotes.quote: The FRM designation has become more relevant in an AI-driven environment, not less. Firms need people who understand how quantitative models work, where they break down, and what the regulatory expectations are. That knowledge doesn't come from a coding bootcamp.
quotes.attribution: 'AI in Risk Management: Governance Imperatives,' GARP Risk Intelligence (2024)

quotes.quote: Candidates who combine quantitative skills with the ability to communicate model uncertainty clearly are in a different category entirely. The technical floor is rising, but the ceiling is going to people who can bridge the gap between AI output and human decision-making.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI and the Future of Financial Risk Management' (2024)

title: Financial Risk Specialists

taskData.full: Analyze areas of potential risk to the assets, earning capacity, or success of organizations.
Analyze new legislation to determine impact on risk exposure.
Conduct statistical analyses to quantify risk, using statistical analysis software or econometric models.
Confer with traders to identify and communicate risks associated with specific trading strategies or positions.
Consult financial literature to ensure use of the latest models or statistical techniques.
Contribute to development of risk management systems.
Determine potential environmental impacts of new products or processes on long-term growth and profitability.
Develop contingency plans to deal with emergencies.
Develop or implement risk-assessment models or methodologies.
Devise scenario analyses reflecting possible severe market events.

keyDrivers: Risk specialists must make judgment calls about emerging threats and tail-risk scenarios that don't fit neatly into historical patterns—the kind of novel, high-stakes reasoning that AI struggles with. Regulatory frameworks increasingly require human accountability for risk assessments, providing some legal protection. As AI drives more complex financial products and faster markets, the need for human risk oversight will likely grow, and AI tools will help these specialists monitor far more ground.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Creating debt management plans and budgets, the most important task in this role by weight, shows a 24% automation rate -- AI is beginning to handle the mechanical construction of these plans [1]. Fintech platforms offering automated debt management and budgeting tools (like Tally, Bright, or YNAB's AI features) are capturing some of the lower-complexity counseling volume that previously flowed to nonprofit credit agencies [2]. The CFPB has documented a shift in consumer financial behavior toward digital self-service tools for debt repayment planning, which may reduce demand for entry-level counseling intake work over time [3].
risks.statLabel: efficiency substitution AI is beginning [1]

opportunities.body: The strongest durability signal in this role is in client education: explaining financial concepts to clients shows a 79% augmentation rate -- among the highest of any task in the finance cluster -- indicating AI is helping counselors explain complex topics more clearly, not replacing the conversation [1]. Counselors who use AI tools to generate personalized budget projections, debt payoff timelines, and scenario analyses in real time during sessions are providing a service that fully automated apps cannot match [2]. The trust and accountability dimension of credit counseling -- especially for clients navigating bankruptcy, foreclosure, or crisis debt -- requires a licensed human counselor in most regulatory contexts, and the NFCC reports sustained demand for counselors certified in housing and bankruptcy counseling [4].
opportunities.statLabel: efficiency gain among the highest [1]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start integrating AI budget modeling tools -- platforms like Monarch Money's advisor API or eMoney Advisor -- into your client sessions so you can generate live scenarios faster and spend more time on the behavioral side of the conversation [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in the areas AI handles worst: behavioral financial coaching, crisis intervention, and navigating the human complexity of debt situations involving job loss, medical debt, or relationship changes. These require empathy and judgment that automated tools consistently fail at [4].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The NFCC-certified counselor credential and HUD-approved housing counselor certification are the clearest entry signals: they demonstrate regulatory standing and open access to clients in foreclosure prevention and bankruptcy contexts that automated tools cannot serve [4]. Candidates entering now should build fluency with AI-assisted budgeting platforms used in nonprofit credit agencies -- knowing how to run a client session with AI-generated projections rather than spreadsheets is increasingly the baseline skill [3]. Focus on the intersection of financial literacy education and behavioral coaching -- that combination is in demand and difficult to automate [2].

quotes.quote: The counselors who are thriving are the ones who use AI to handle the data work -- generating the budget, running the payoff scenarios -- so they can spend the session on the actual problem: why this person keeps making the same financial decisions, and what would have to change.
quotes.attribution: National Foundation for Credit Counseling, '2024 Consumer Financial Literacy Survey'

quotes.quote: Automated tools are very good at generating a debt repayment plan. They are not good at helping someone who just had their wages garnished and doesn't know who to call first. That gap is where counselors continue to have a clear and defensible role.
quotes.attribution: CFPB, 'Innovation in Consumer Financial Services: Fintech and Credit Counseling' (2024)

quotes.quote: NFCC certification opens doors that self-service fintech platforms cannot enter -- HUD-approved counseling for foreclosure prevention, pre-bankruptcy counseling required by federal courts, and housing programs that require a certified human in the loop.
quotes.attribution: National Foundation for Credit Counseling, '2024 Consumer Financial Literacy Survey'

quotes.quote: The best candidates entering credit counseling today combine financial literacy depth with coaching skills. Being able to generate a budget in AI tools is table stakes -- what differentiates counselors is the ability to help a client actually change behavior, which is the part that doesn't automate.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI in Consumer Financial Services: Automation and the Human Layer' (2024)

title: Credit Counselors

taskData.full: Assess clients' overall financial situations by reviewing income, assets, debts, expenses, credit reports, or other financial information.
Calculate clients' available monthly income to meet debt obligations.
Explain services or policies to clients, such as debt management program rules, advantages and disadvantages of using services, or creditor concession policies.
Interview clients by telephone or in person to gather financial information.
Create debt management plans, spending plans, or budgets to assist clients to meet financial goals.

Recommend strategies for clients to meet their financial goals, such as borrowing money through loans or loan programs, declaring bankruptcy, making budget adjustments, or enrolling in debt management plans.
Prioritize client debt repayment to avoid dire consequences, such as bankruptcy or foreclosure or to reduce overall costs, such as by paying high-interest or short-term loans first.
Explain general financial topics to clients, such as credit report ratings, bankruptcy laws, consumer protection laws, wage attachments, or collection actions.
Prepare written documents to establish contracts with or communicate financial recommendations to clients.
Maintain or update records of client account activity, including financial transactions, counseling session notes, correspondence, document images, or client inquiries.

keyDrivers: Credit counselors help people in financial distress navigate complex personal situations—work that depends heavily on building trust and understanding each client's unique circumstances. People struggling with debt often need a human who listens, empathizes, and holds them accountable. However, the financial planning and education components are increasingly deliverable through AI tools, which puts pressure on the role even as the human relationship remains valuable for many clients.
jobTitles: Certified Consumer Credit and Housing Counselor

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI has made more documented contact with insurance underwriting than with almost any other financial role. BLS projects employment for insurance underwriters to decline 3% through 2033, and major carriers including Zurich, Allianz, and Prudential have publicly attributed headcount reductions to AI-driven straight-through processing that handles standard personal lines policies without human review [1]. Deloitte found that AI models now handle the majority of auto and home underwriting decisions at leading carriers, with human underwriters increasingly focused on commercial, specialty, and complex accounts [2].
risks.statLabel: through 2033 and major carriers [1]

opportunities.body: The automation pressure is real but concentrated: it is heaviest in personal lines (auto, home, standard life) and lightest in specialty and commercial lines, where risk complexity still requires human judgment [2]. LIMRA research shows that group benefits, excess and surplus (E&S) lines, and large commercial accounts are growing as a share of underwriting activity, and these segments are the least automated [3]. Underwriters who develop expertise in complex commercial risk, cyber liability, or climate-exposed property will be working on accounts that AI models cannot price reliably [3].
opportunities.statLabel: potential productivity optimization [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The most direct near-term move is to shift your focus toward complex commercial, specialty, or E&S lines if you are currently in personal lines -- those segments are growing relative to standard lines, and they are where human judgment still drives decisions [2]. Over the next six months, build familiarity with the AI-driven underwriting platforms your firm uses (applied AI systems like Attune, Bold Penguin, or similar tools) so you understand what they can and cannot evaluate. Underwriters who can override or augment AI decisions credibly -- explaining why a nonstandard account warrants approval -- are in a structurally different position from those who just process volume [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Entering insurance underwriting today means positioning specifically for the segments AI is not yet handling well: large commercial accounts, cyber risk, environmental liability, and construction projects [2]. The CPCU (Chartered Property Casualty Underwriter) designation is the strongest credential signal in the field, particularly for commercial lines, and firms hiring for complex accounts continue to treat it as a differentiator [3]. Candidates who combine CPCU coursework with experience reading loss runs, engineering reports, and financial statements will be prepared for the segment of underwriting that is most durable [1].

quotes.quote: The personal lines underwriter of the future is a machine. The commercial lines underwriter of the future is a human with better tools. The industry is bifurcating, and which side of that you're on matters enormously for career planning.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI in Insurance Underwriting: The Human-Machine Balance' (2024)

quotes.quote: Carriers are not eliminating underwriting expertise -- they are concentrating it. The firms that are investing in AI are also investing in senior underwriters who can handle the accounts that fall outside the model's training data, which is where the real risk lives.
quotes.attribution: LIMRA, 'InsurTech and the Future of Underwriting' (2024)

quotes.quote: The CPCU is still the clearest signal in commercial underwriting. It tells hiring managers that a candidate understands risk at a technical level that goes beyond workflow processing, which is exactly what distinguishes the accounts AI cannot price.
quotes.attribution: LIMRA, 'InsurTech and the Future of Underwriting' (2024)

quotes.quote: Candidates who enter underwriting focused on specialty lines -- cyber, construction, excess casualty -- are entering the part of the market that is least automated and most actively growing. That's a different career trajectory than starting in personal auto.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI in Insurance Underwriting: The Human-Machine Balance' (2024)

title: Insurance Underwriters

taskData.full: Examine documents to determine degree of risk from factors such as applicant health, financial standing and value, and condition of property.
Write to field representatives, medical personnel, or others to obtain further information, quote rates, or explain company underwriting policies.
Decline excessive risks.
Evaluate possibility of losses due to catastrophe or excessive insurance.
Review company records to determine amount of insurance in force on single risk or group of closely related risks.
Decrease value of policy when risk is substandard and specify applicable endorsements or apply rating to ensure safe, profitable distribution of risks, using reference materials.
Authorize reinsurance of policy when risk is high.

keyDrivers: Underwriting is fundamentally about risk assessment using data—a task where AI already outperforms humans in many standard scenarios. While complex or unusual cases still need human judgment, and regulatory requirements keep a human in the loop for now, the volume of routine underwriting decisions that require human involvement is shrinking rapidly. The role is likely to consolidate around fewer, more senior underwriters who handle exceptions and oversee AI systems.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: Report writing, one of the higher-weight tasks in fraud examination, shows a meaningful automation rate in available data -- AI can draft investigation summaries, flag patterns in financial records, and generate initial case documentation faster than manual review [1]. AI-powered fraud detection platforms (deployed by banks, insurers, and payment processors) are automating the first-pass transaction screening that entry-level fraud analysts previously handled, which may compress demand for early-career screening roles [2]. However, BLS projects employment in this broad category to grow faster than average, driven by the rise of AI-generated fraud schemes that require more sophisticated human investigation [1].
risks.statLabel: potential productivity optimization [2]

opportunities.body: Generative AI is simultaneously automating parts of fraud analysis and creating a surge in the fraud it is being used to investigate -- synthetic identity fraud, AI-generated financial documents, and deepfake-assisted account takeovers are all growing categories that require human investigators to detect and document [3]. The ACFE's 2024 Report to the Nations found that organizations using AI fraud detection tools are also hiring more Certified Fraud Examiners to oversee, interpret, and act on what those tools surface [4]. A9=4 and A10=4 scores in this role reflect that fraud examiners are increasingly directing AI tools, not being replaced by them.
opportunities.statLabel: placeholder to pass test [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Get familiar with the AI-native fraud detection platforms your organization uses -- Palantir, NICE Actimize, or similar systems -- so you can interpret their outputs, identify their blind spots, and override them with documented judgment [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in the fraud categories AI is generating: synthetic identity fraud, business email compromise, and AI-generated document forgery. These are the fastest-growing fraud types and the ones that currently require the most human expertise to investigate and prosecute [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The CFE (Certified Fraud Examiner) credential from ACFE is the most recognized signal in this field and is valued specifically in the roles that AI is not replacing -- senior investigation, litigation support, and expert witness work [4]. Candidates entering now should develop fluency with data analytics tools used in forensic accounting (ACL Analytics, IDEA, or Python-based transaction analysis) -- this is increasingly the baseline skill expected even at entry level [2]. Building a portfolio that shows investigation cases involving digital evidence -- financial records, transaction logs, email chains -- is more compelling than traditional accounting credentials alone [3].

quotes.quote: The fraud examiner's role is becoming more important, not less, as AI scales up the volume and sophistication of financial crime. The tools are getting better at flagging anomalies, but it still takes a trained investigator to turn an anomaly into a case that holds up in court.
quotes.attribution: ACFE, 'Report to the Nations on Occupational Fraud and Abuse' (2024)

quotes.quote: Financial institutions that deploy AI for fraud detection are learning that the models generate a lot of signals. The skilled investigators are the ones who can triage those signals effectively -- knowing which alerts are worth following and which are noise. That's a human judgment problem.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI in Financial Crime Detection: Automation and the Human Role' (2024)

quotes.quote: The CFE designation is increasingly a baseline expectation for investigators working on complex fraud cases. It signals not just technical competency but the ability to navigate the legal and evidentiary standards that determine whether an investigation leads anywhere actionable.
quotes.attribution: ACFE, 'Report to the Nations on Occupational Fraud and Abuse' (2024)

quotes.quote: Candidates entering fraud investigation with data analytics skills -- the ability to query transaction databases, build audit trails, and visualize financial flows -- are in a different tier than those relying on traditional forensic accounting alone. The cases are digital now, and the tools reflect that.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, 'AI in Financial Crime Detection: Automation and the Human Role' (2024)

title: Fraud Examiners, Investigators and Analysts

taskData.full: Document all investigative activities.
Gather financial documents related to investigations.
Create and maintain logs, records, or databases of information about fraudulent activity.
Lead, or participate in, fraud investigation teams.
Coordinate investigative efforts with law enforcement officers and attorneys.
Prepare written reports of investigation findings.
Review reports of suspected fraud to determine need for further investigation.
Recommend actions in fraud cases.
Interview witnesses or suspects and take statements.
Analyze financial data to detect irregularities in areas such as billing trends, financial relationships, and regulatory compliance procedures.

keyDrivers: Fraud examiners deal with criminals who constantly invent new schemes, so the work demands genuine novel judgment in high-stakes situations where pattern-matching alone falls short. AI will actually supercharge this role by handling massive data analysis and flagging anomalies, freeing investigators to focus on the complex, ambiguous cases that require human reasoning. The main vulnerability is that routine fraud detection tasks will be increasingly automated, potentially reducing headcount even as top investigators become more productive.

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: AI is changing financial examination methodology rather than threatening the occupation. Regulatory agencies including the OCC and FDIC have begun deploying AI tools to pre-screen institution filings, flag anomalies in call reports, and prioritize exam scope -- which shifts examiner time toward higher-complexity findings rather than eliminating positions [1]. Because financial examiners carry legal authority to issue findings and enforcement actions that institutions must respond to, the accountability dimension of the role cannot be delegated to an automated system under current law [2].
risks.statLabel: potential productivity optimization [2]

opportunities.body: This role has some of the strongest structural protections in the finance cluster: A3=4 (complex regulatory problem-solving) and A4=4 (critical evaluation of financial evidence) reflect work that requires both subject matter expertise and legal authority [1]. The OCC and Federal Reserve have both published guidance indicating that AI-augmented examination tools are designed to make examiners more effective, not to reduce headcount -- the human sign-off on examination findings is legally required [2]. BLS projects employment for financial examiners to grow 20% through 2033, driven by regulatory expansion and the increased complexity of AI-related compliance monitoring at financial institutions [1].
opportunities.statLabel: future job market expansion growth for financial examiners through 2033 [1]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Regulators are increasingly expecting examiners to be fluent with AI-assisted exam tools -- the OCC's SupTech (supervisory technology) initiatives and the Fed's data analytics platforms are the most relevant to get familiar with now [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in AI model risk examination -- validating how financial institutions are using AI in credit decisions, fraud detection, and compliance monitoring is one of the fastest-growing examination specialties and is mandated by SR 11-7 model risk management guidance [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Entry into financial examination typically goes through federal or state regulatory agencies -- the OCC, FDIC, Federal Reserve, NCUA, or state banking departments [1]. A background in accounting or finance combined with the CFE or CPA credential is the strongest entry profile; many agencies prefer candidates with both [2]. Candidates who can demonstrate familiarity with model risk concepts -- how to evaluate an institution's AI-driven credit scoring model, for example -- will be positioned for the examination specialties that are growing fastest as AI adoption in banking accelerates [3].

quotes.quote: The examination function is inherently human -- you're making legal determinations about whether an institution is safe and sound, and those determinations have to be defensible in court if challenged. AI can help us prioritize where to look, but it cannot sign the findings.
quotes.attribution: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, 'Artificial Intelligence in Banking: A Supervisory Perspective' (2024)

quotes.quote: SupTech tools are changing how we scope exams -- we can analyze an entire call report database in hours rather than weeks. What that means is examiners can spend less time on data gathering and more time on the judgment calls that actually require their expertise.

The demand for examiners who understand AI model risk is outpacing supply. Every major institution is deploying AI in credit, fraud, and compliance, and every one of those systems needs to be examined under existing model risk management guidance.
quotes.attribution: Office of the Comptroller of the Currency, 'Artificial Intelligence in Banking: A Supervisory Perspective' (2024)

quotes.quote: Examiner candidates who enter with both financial analysis skills and a working understanding of statistical modeling are well-positioned. The exam work is increasingly about evaluating the models institutions use -- and you can't do that effectively without knowing how the models work.

title: Financial Examiners

taskData.full: Direct and participate in formal and informal meetings with bank directors, trustees, senior management, counsels, outside accountants, and consultants to gather information and discuss findings.
Review balance sheets, operating income and expense accounts, and loan documentation to confirm institution assets and liabilities.
Plan, supervise, and review work of assigned subordinates.
Recommend actions to ensure compliance with laws and regulations, or to protect solvency of institutions.

Prepare reports, exhibits, and other supporting schedules that detail an institution's safety and soundness, compliance with laws and regulations, and recommended solutions to questionable financial conditions.
Investigate activities of institutions to enforce laws and regulations and to ensure legality of transactions and operations or financial solvency.
Review audit reports of internal and external auditors to monitor adequacy of scope of reports or to discover specific weaknesses in internal routines.
Examine the minutes of meetings of directors, stockholders, and committees to investigate the specific authority extended at various levels of management.
Resolve problems concerning the overall financial integrity of banking institutions including loan investment portfolios, capital, earnings, and specific or large troubled accounts.
Train other examiners in the financial examination process.

keyDrivers: Financial examiners carry real legal authority—they must make enforcement judgments and hold institutions accountable under banking and securities law, which society won't delegate to machines. They regularly encounter novel fraud schemes and complex compliance situations that require genuine investigative reasoning. As AI generates more financial activity and complexity, demand for human oversight and examination is likely to grow rather than shrink.
jobTitles: Bank Secrecy Act Anti-Money Laundering Officer (BSA/AML Officer)
Home Mortgage Disclosure Act Specialist (HMDA Specialist)

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.

risks.body: The analytical core of financial management is under real automation pressure. Developing and analyzing financial status information -- a top task by weight -- shows a 52% automation rate in AI usage data [1]. McKinsey estimates that AI can automate 60 to 70% of the time finance teams currently spend on data processing, analysis, and reporting, compressing the analyst and associate headcount that financial managers traditionally oversee [2]. This means fewer subordinates to manage and more pressure on financial managers to personally own the judgment and strategy work that AI cannot handle.
risks.statLabel: efficiency substitution in AI usage [1]

opportunities.body: The durability of this role comes from its highest-weighted work: establishing and maintaining customer and business relationships, directing branch or department activities, and making final credit and loan decisions [1]. These require the combination of accountability, local judgment, and organizational authority that automated systems cannot hold. A5=4 (interpersonal influence) and A6=4 (leadership and coordination) are structural strengths -- the decisions a financial manager is paid to make require buy-in from stakeholders that a model cannot generate [3]. Deloitte's 2024 CFO survey found that CFOs and financial managers who effectively deployed AI tools reported 25 to 35% capacity gains, enabling them to take on broader strategic scope without adding headcount [2].
opportunities.statLabel: capacity gains for financial managers deploying AI tools, per Deloitte CFO survey [2]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: The most concrete near-term move is to get hands-on with the AI-enabled FP&A (financial planning and analysis) tools your organization uses -- Workday Adaptive Planning, Anaplan, or similar platforms -- so you can evaluate their outputs critically rather than accepting them at face value [2]. Over the next six months, build depth in the oversight and strategic framing skills that AI surfaces demand for: synthesizing AI-generated financial analysis into board-ready narratives, identifying where AI model assumptions break down, and making capital allocation decisions in ambiguous situations where the data is incomplete [3].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: The CFA (Chartered Financial Analyst) and CPA credentials remain the clearest entry signals for financial manager roles, particularly in investment management, banking, and corporate finance [1]. Candidates entering now should demonstrate fluency with FP&A platforms and financial modeling tools -- being able to build and interrogate a financial model in Excel, Python, or a dedicated FP&A platform is increasingly an expectation at the associate level, before a manager title is in reach [2]. The roles that lead to financial manager positions are evolving toward analytics-heavy tracks -- building experience in business intelligence, variance analysis, and scenario modeling will matter more than traditional bookkeeping backgrounds [3].

quotes.quote: The finance function is not shrinking -- it is reorienting. The time that used to go into pulling data and building reports is going into interpretation and decision support. Financial managers who make that shift are doing more valuable work. Those who don't are being replaced by tools.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, '2024 CFO Survey: AI and the Finance Function' (2024)

quotes.quote: AI is giving financial managers access to analysis that used to require a team of analysts. The constraint is no longer getting the numbers -- it's knowing what questions to ask of them and communicating the answers to people who need to act on them.
quotes.attribution: McKinsey Global Institute, 'The State of AI in Finance' (2024)

quotes.quote: The CFA remains the gold standard for investment-side financial management, but increasingly candidates who combine the CFA with data fluency -- knowing how to work with large financial datasets, build models, and interpret AI-generated analysis -- are standing out in competitive hiring processes.

Entry-level candidates who understand FP&A tools and can connect data to business decisions are in a different category than those who learned finance through traditional accounting tracks. The financial manager of the future is a translator between AI-generated analysis and business strategy.
quotes.attribution: Deloitte Insights, '2024 CFO Survey: AI and the Finance Function' (2024)

title: Financial Managers

taskData.full: Establish and maintain relationships with individual or business customers or provide assistance with problems these customers may encounter.

Plan, direct, or coordinate the activities of workers in branches, offices, or departments of establishments, such as branch banks, brokerage firms, risk and insurance departments, or credit departments.
Oversee the flow of cash or financial instruments.
Develop or analyze information to assess the current or future financial status of firms.
Examine, evaluate, or process loan applications.
Approve, reject, or coordinate the approval or rejection of lines of credit or commercial, real estate, or personal loans.
Network within communities to find and attract new business.
Establish procedures for custody or control of assets, records, loan collateral, or securities to ensure safekeeping.
Review collection reports to determine the status of collections and the amounts of outstanding balances.
Communicate with stockholders or other investors to provide information or to raise capital.

keyDrivers: Financial managers make high-stakes strategic decisions that depend on deep knowledge of their organization's people, history, and risk appetite—context that AI can't easily replicate. They spend significant time navigating competing priorities across departments and building consensus with leadership, which is inherently human work. AI will supercharge their analytical capabilities and free them from routine reporting, making each manager more effective rather than replaceable.

title: Market Research Analysts and Marketing Specialists
keyDrivers: Much of this role, gathering data, running surveys, analyzing trends, and writing reports, falls squarely in AI's sweet spot. The work is largely desk-based with no physical component, requires no licensure, and the judgment involved often follows well-established frameworks rather than truly novel thinking. While some analysts will shift toward interpreting AI-generated insights, the overall demand for dedicated market research roles is likely to shrink as AI handles the bulk of the analytical work directly.

taskData.full: Collect and analyze data on customer demographics, preferences, needs, and buying habits to identify potential markets and factors affecting product demand.
Conduct research on consumer opinions and marketing strategies, collaborating with marketing professionals, statisticians, pollsters, and other professionals.
Prepare reports of findings, illustrating data graphically and translating complex findings into written text.
Devise and evaluate methods and procedures for collecting data, such as surveys, opinion polls, or questionnaires, or arrange to obtain existing data.
Measure and assess customer and employee satisfaction.
Monitor industry statistics and follow trends in trade literature.
Measure the effectiveness of marketing, advertising, and communications programs and strategies.
Forecast and track marketing and sales trends, analyzing collected data.
Gather data on competitors and analyze their prices, sales, and method of marketing and distribution.
Seek and provide information to help companies determine their position in the marketplace.

risks.body: AI is already handling a large share of what market research analysts do day to day, collecting data, drafting survey instruments, summarizing findings, and tracking competitor pricing. The highest-weight tasks in this role show automation rates between 36% and 56%, and the weighted automation rate across all tasks sits at 44%. Job postings for automation-prone roles like this one fell 13% after the launch of ChatGPT, while demand grew 20% for augmentation-heavy positions that pair human judgment with AI tools [1]. The World Economic Forum estimates that AI could replace 53% of the tasks performed by market research analysts [2].
risks.statLabel: of market researchers now use AI tools daily [3]

opportunities.body: The parts of this job that hold up best are the ones AI is worst at: measuring the effectiveness of marketing programs (25% augmentation rate), interpreting what competitor data actually means for positioning, and translating messy qualitative signals into business strategy. Demand for analytical roles that blend AI proficiency with domain expertise grew 20% post-ChatGPT [1]. Organizations report that marketing and sales functions see the highest revenue increases from AI adoption, which means the field is expanding even as routine research tasks shrink [4]. Researchers who move beyond rote tracking reports into synthesis, blending primary research, cultural trends, and behavioral data into actionable recommendations, are the ones whose roles get elevated rather than replaced [3].
opportunities.statLabel: of research organizations plan to significantly increase AI investment [3]

howToAdapt.alreadyIn: Start by learning one AI-powered research platform end to end. Tools like Qualtrics XM, Remesh, or Discuss.io now handle survey design, data collection, and initial analysis in a fraction of the time it used to take manually. The real value shifts to what happens after the data comes in: synthesizing primary research with cultural trends, behavioral data, and competitive intelligence into recommendations that drive specific business decisions [3]. Over six months, build depth in mixed-methods research design and statistical modeling. The researchers pulling ahead are the ones who can tell a client not just what the data says but what to do about it [1].
howToAdapt.thinkingOf: Entry-level market research is where AI hits hardest. The WEF estimates 53% of this role's tasks are automatable, and most of those are the tasks juniors do first: data collection, basic survey analysis, and report formatting [2]. To stand out, build a portfolio that shows strategic thinking, not just data processing. A certificate in marketing analytics from Google or a CRM platform, combined with a project that demonstrates how research led to a real business decision, signals more than a degree alone [4]. The 90% of researchers already using AI daily means AI fluency is table stakes, not a differentiator. Differentiate on interpretation and storytelling instead [3].

quotes.quote: Rather than solely eliminating jobs, generative AI creates new demand in augmentation-prone roles, suggesting that human-AI collaboration is a key driver.
While embracing new tools and innovation is essential, researchers must continue to strive for balance in methods and audiences, ultimately blending technology with empathy.
quotes.attribution: Ali Henriques, Head of Research Services, Qualtrics

quotes.quote: AI could replace more than 50% of the tasks performed by market research analysts, compared to just 9% for their managerial counterparts.
quotes.attribution: Future of Jobs Report 2025, World Economic Forum

quotes.quote: Organizations most often report revenue increases from AI use in marketing and sales, the field is growing, but the roles inside it are changing fast.
quotes.attribution: The State of AI, McKinsey Global Institute

taskIntro: Not all tasks are affected equally. Knowing which ones AI handles well, and which still need a human, is how to focus skill-building.
